name: OpenClaw Gateway Token Theft
description: OpenClaw Control UI trusts gatewayUrl query parameter without validation, allowing token theft via crafted links.
remediation: Update OpenClaw to version 2026.1.29 or later. Validate gateway URLs before connecting.

name: ClawHub Credential Stealer -- env-dump skill
description: Malicious ClawHub skill that exfiltrates all environment variables including API keys and tokens to a remote endpoint.
remediation: Remove the skill immediately. Rotate all API keys and tokens that were present in the environment.

name: ClawHub Credential Stealer -- keychain-read skill
description: Malicious ClawHub skill that reads system keychain entries and exfiltrates stored credentials.
remediation: Remove the skill immediately. Change all passwords stored in the system keychain.

name: ClawHub Credential Stealer -- ssh-key-grab skill
description: Malicious ClawHub skill that reads and exfiltrates SSH private keys from the user home directory.
remediation: Remove the skill immediately. Regenerate all SSH keys and revoke compromised public keys from authorized hosts.

name: ClawHub Env Exfiltrator -- dotenv-sync skill
description: Malicious ClawHub skill that searches for .env files across the filesystem and uploads their contents to a remote server.
remediation: Remove the skill. Rotate all secrets found in .env files on the system.

name: Supply Chain -- Suspicious npm package (hack pattern)
description: MCP server launched via npm package with suspicious name containing 'hack'. Likely malicious supply chain package.
remediation: Remove the MCP server configuration. Verify the package source on npmjs.com before reinstalling.

name: Supply Chain -- Suspicious npm package (exfil pattern)
description: MCP server launched via npm package with suspicious name containing 'exfil'. Likely a data exfiltration tool.
remediation: Remove the MCP server configuration immediately. Audit system for signs of data exfiltration.

name: Supply Chain -- Suspicious npm package (backdoor pattern)
description: MCP server launched via npm package with suspicious name containing 'backdoor' or 'pwn'. Likely contains a remote access backdoor.
remediation: Remove the MCP server configuration immediately. Scan system for persistence mechanisms. Consider reimaging the host.

name: RCE via Command Injection -- semicolon in args
description: MCP server arguments contain shell command injection via semicolon separator. Attacker can execute arbitrary commands.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Never pass untrusted input as command arguments. Sanitize all args for shell metacharacters.

name: RCE via Command Injection -- pipe in args
description: MCP server arguments contain shell command injection via pipe operator. Attacker can chain arbitrary commands.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Audit argument handling for shell injection vulnerabilities.

name: RCE via Command Injection -- backtick or subshell in args
description: MCP server arguments contain shell command injection via subshell or backtick execution. Attacker can execute arbitrary commands.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Ensure arguments are never passed through a shell interpreter.

name: Dangerous Tool -- Unrestricted filesystem read access
description: MCP server requests unrestricted read access to the entire filesystem. This can expose sensitive files including credentials and private keys.
remediation: Restrict filesystem access to specific directories. Use the principle of least privilege for file access permissions.

name: Dangerous Tool -- Unrestricted network access
description: MCP server requests unrestricted network access. This can be used to exfiltrate data or establish reverse shells.
remediation: Restrict network access to specific hosts and ports. Monitor outbound connections from MCP servers.

name: Dangerous Tool -- Process execution without justification
description: MCP server has unrestricted process execution capability. This bypasses all sandboxing and allows arbitrary command execution.
remediation: Restrict execution to specific binaries. Use allowlists for permitted commands.

name: Dangerous Tool -- Clipboard and screenshot access
description: MCP server accesses clipboard contents or captures screenshots. This can expose sensitive data copied by the user.
remediation: Remove the server unless clipboard/screenshot access is explicitly required. Monitor for data exfiltration.

name: Docker Escape -- Privileged container mount
description: MCP server arguments request privileged Docker access or host PID namespace. This enables container escape and full host compromise.
remediation: Never run MCP servers with --privileged flag. Remove Docker socket mounts. Use rootless containers.

name: Docker Escape -- Host filesystem mount
description: MCP server mounts the host root filesystem or sensitive host directories into a container. This allows reading and modifying host system files.
remediation: Restrict volume mounts to specific application directories. Never mount / or /etc into containers.

name: Docker Escape -- CAP_SYS_ADMIN capability
description: MCP server container requests SYS_ADMIN or ALL capabilities. This grants near-root privileges and enables container escape.
remediation: Drop all capabilities and add back only what is strictly needed. Use --cap-drop=ALL --cap-add=<specific>.

name: Docker Escape -- Host network namespace
description: MCP server container uses host network namespace. This bypasses network isolation and exposes all host network interfaces.
remediation: Use bridge or custom Docker networks instead of host networking. Restrict container network access.

name: Docker Escape -- Cgroup escape via release_agent
description: MCP server arguments reference cgroup release_agent mechanism. This is a known container escape technique.
remediation: Remove the MCP server immediately. Audit the host for signs of compromise. Use cgroup v2 with restricted permissions.

name: mcp-remote RCE via OAuth URL injection
description: mcp-remote versions before 0.4.0 pass malicious OAuth endpoint URLs directly to the shell, enabling arbitrary OS command execution when connecting to untrusted servers. Affected 437,000+ downloads.
remediation: Update mcp-remote to version 0.4.0 or later. Never connect to untrusted MCP servers with vulnerable versions.

name: Figma MCP Server command injection
description: Critical command injection vulnerability in the Figma MCP server affecting 600,000+ downloads. Attacker-controlled input is passed unsanitized to shell execution.
remediation: Update to the latest patched version of the Figma MCP server. Validate all input parameters before shell execution.

name: mcp-atlassian unauthenticated RCE and SSRF
description: Critical unauthenticated RCE and SSRF via path traversal in Confluence attachment downloads. Allows arbitrary file write and internal network access.
remediation: Update mcp-atlassian to the latest patched version. Restrict network access and validate all file paths.

name: Anthropic filesystem server path validation bypass
description: Path validation bypass in official Anthropic filesystem MCP server. When chained with git server vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-68143, CVE-2025-68144), enables full RCE via malicious .git/config files.
remediation: Update to the latest version of the filesystem server. Restrict allowed directories to the minimum needed.

name: Anthropic git server argument injection via git_diff
description: Argument injection in git_diff tool and unrestricted git_init in official Anthropic git MCP server. Combined with filesystem server, enables RCE through crafted .git/config.
remediation: Update to the latest version of the git server. Sanitize all arguments passed to git commands.

name: Azure MCP Server SSRF via OAuth proxy
description: SSRF vulnerability in Azure MCP Server. Trusts X-Atlassian headers and OAuth proxy requests without authorization boundary validation, enabling unauthorized access to internal services.
remediation: Update Azure MCP Server to the latest version. Implement strict authorization boundaries for OAuth proxy requests.

name: NeighborJack -- MCP server bound to 0.0.0.0
description: MCP server explicitly bound to all network interfaces (0.0.0.0). Any device on the same local network can access the server without authentication, enabling unauthorized tool execution.
remediation: Bind MCP servers to 127.0.0.1 (localhost) only. Use firewall rules to restrict access. Add authentication to all exposed endpoints.

name: Supply Chain -- Malicious PyPI MCP package (reverse shell)
description: Malicious MCP server package from PyPI containing reverse shell functionality. Establishes outbound connection to attacker-controlled infrastructure upon installation.
remediation: Remove the package immediately. Check for active reverse shell connections. Audit system for persistence mechanisms and compromised credentials.

name: Supply Chain -- Suspicious pip MCP package (exfil/hack/backdoor)
description: MCP server installed via pip/pipx with suspicious package name indicating malicious intent. Python MCP packages on PyPI have been used to deploy reverse shells and data exfiltration tools.
remediation: Remove the package immediately. Verify package source and author on PyPI. Scan system for signs of compromise.

name: Postmark MCP Server impersonation (email exfiltration)
description: Impersonation of legitimate Postmark email service MCP server. Injects BCC copies of all sent emails to attacker-controlled addresses, exfiltrating confidential documents, memos, and invoices.
remediation: Remove the server immediately. Verify the package source against the official Postmark documentation. Audit sent emails for unauthorized BCC recipients.

name: SSRF via MarkItDown MCP Server (cloud metadata theft)
description: SSRF vulnerability in MarkItDown MCP server affecting 36.7% of analyzed cloud deployments. Enables exploitation of cloud metadata services (AWS EC2 169.254.169.254) to steal IAM credentials and access tokens.
remediation: Update to the latest patched version. Block access to cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254). Run MCP servers with IMDSv2 enforcement on AWS.

name: Azure Data Explorer KQL injection
description: KQL (Kusto Query Language) injection vulnerability in Azure Data Explorer MCP server. Unvalidated query parameters enable data exfiltration and unauthorized query execution.
remediation: Update to the latest version. Parameterize all KQL queries. Implement query validation and sandboxing.

name: RCE via base64-encoded command injection in args
description: MCP server arguments contain base64-encoded command injection. Attacker obfuscates malicious commands using base64 encoding to evade simple pattern detection.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Never pass base64-decoded content directly to shell execution. Audit all argument handling for injection vulnerabilities.

name: Claude Code WebSocket authentication bypass
description: Authentication bypass in Claude Code IDE extensions via WebSocket. Allows unauthenticated local connections to execute tools with the user's full privileges.
remediation: Update Claude Code extensions to the latest version. Ensure WebSocket connections require authentication tokens.

name: Supply Chain -- Suspicious npm MCP package (shell/exec pattern)
description: MCP server launched via npm package with name indicating unrestricted shell or command execution capability. These packages often grant full system access without sandboxing.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Verify the package source and audit its code before use. Prefer MCP servers with explicit permission scoping.

name: WhatsApp MCP Server chat history exfiltration
description: Tool poisoning vulnerability in WhatsApp MCP server enables attackers to inject malicious instructions that exfiltrate entire chat histories via crafted tool descriptions.
remediation: Update to the latest patched version. Review tool descriptions for hidden instructions. Restrict MCP server access to specific chat scopes.

name: Supabase MCP SQL injection via support tickets
description: SQL injection attack vector in Supabase MCP server. Attackers embed SQL instructions in support tickets that agents process, enabling exfiltration of integration tokens and database credentials.
remediation: Update to the latest version. Use parameterized queries. Sanitize all user-provided content before passing to MCP tools. Restrict database permissions to read-only where possible.

name: Anthropic git server unrestricted git_init (CVE-2025-68143)
description: Unrestricted git_init tool in official Anthropic git MCP server can turn sensitive directories (e.g. .ssh) into git repositories, enabling exfiltration of private keys via git operations.
remediation: Update to the latest version of the git server. Restrict git_init to approved directories only.

name: Supply Chain -- Typosquatted MCP npm package
description: MCP server installed via npm package with a name resembling common typos of legitimate MCP packages. Typosquatting is a known supply chain attack vector for distributing malware through package registries.
remediation: Verify the exact package name against the official repository. Remove any typosquatted packages immediately. Run npm audit to check for known vulnerabilities.

name: Supply Chain -- Typosquatted MCP PyPI package
description: MCP server installed via pip with a name resembling common typos of legitimate MCP packages. Typosquatting on PyPI has been used to distribute reverse shells and credential stealers targeting MCP users.
remediation: Verify the exact package name against the official PyPI listing. Remove any typosquatted packages. Check for active reverse shell connections.

name: HTTP transport without TLS for remote MCP server
description: MCP server configured to use unencrypted HTTP transport. All tool invocations, credentials, and data are transmitted in plaintext, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks and credential interception.
remediation: Switch to HTTPS for all remote MCP server connections. Enable TLS certificate validation. Use mTLS for high-security environments.

name: RCE via Command Injection -- ampersand chaining in args
description: MCP server arguments contain shell command injection via ampersand operator chaining. Attacker can chain arbitrary commands after the intended command completes.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Sanitize all arguments for shell metacharacters including &, &&, and other chaining operators.

name: Docker Escape -- seccomp disabled
description: MCP server container runs with seccomp or AppArmor security profiles disabled. This removes kernel-level syscall filtering and mandatory access controls, enabling container escape.
remediation: Remove --security-opt overrides. Use the default seccomp and AppArmor profiles. Apply custom restrictive profiles if the default is too permissive.

name: Slack MCP Server message exfiltration
description: Slack MCP server with overly broad OAuth scopes can be exploited to exfiltrate channel messages, DMs, and files. Tool poisoning can inject instructions to silently forward messages to attacker-controlled channels.
remediation: Restrict OAuth scopes to minimum required permissions. Review tool descriptions for hidden exfiltration instructions. Monitor Slack API usage for anomalous access patterns.

name: GitHub MCP Server token scope abuse
description: GitHub MCP server configured with overly broad personal access token scopes. Tool poisoning can exploit write permissions to push malicious code, create backdoor branches, or modify CI/CD workflows.
remediation: Use fine-grained personal access tokens with minimal scopes. Restrict to specific repositories. Prefer GitHub Apps with installation-level permissions over PATs.

name: Database MCP Server SQL injection via tool parameters
description: Database MCP servers that accept raw SQL queries from LLM agents without parameterization. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into executing DROP, DELETE, or data exfiltration queries.
remediation: Use parameterized queries exclusively. Restrict database user to read-only permissions. Implement query allowlists. Never grant DDL privileges to MCP server database users.

name: Supply Chain -- npm install script execution
description: MCP server installation explicitly enables npm lifecycle scripts. Malicious packages use postinstall scripts to execute arbitrary code during installation, before any security review can occur.
remediation: Use --ignore-scripts when installing untrusted MCP packages. Audit package.json scripts before installation. Use npm audit to check for known vulnerabilities.

name: Cloud metadata SSRF via MCP tool parameters
description: MCP server arguments reference cloud metadata service endpoints. This enables SSRF attacks to steal IAM credentials, access tokens, and instance metadata from AWS, GCP, or Alibaba Cloud environments.
remediation: Block access to cloud metadata endpoints at the network level. Enforce IMDSv2 on AWS. Run MCP servers in isolated network segments without metadata service access.

name: MCP server with OAuth static client secret
description: MCP server configured with a static OAuth client secret in command-line arguments. Secrets in process arguments are visible via /proc and process listing, enabling credential theft.
remediation: Use environment variables or secure credential stores for OAuth secrets. Implement PKCE for public clients. Rotate compromised client secrets immediately.

name: MCP server with disabled certificate validation
description: MCP server configured to skip TLS certificate validation. This enables man-in-the-middle attacks where attackers can intercept and modify all traffic between the client and server, including credentials and tool responses.
remediation: Enable TLS certificate validation. Use trusted CA certificates. If using self-signed certificates, configure the specific CA bundle rather than disabling validation entirely.

name: Anthropic Filesystem MCP Server sandbox escape (CVE-2025-53109/53110)
description: Sandbox escape vulnerabilities in Anthropic Filesystem MCP Server (versions prior to 0.6.3 / 2025.7.1). Attackers can escape the server sandbox, tamper with arbitrary files on the host, and execute arbitrary code via symlink following and path traversal.
remediation: Update @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem to version 2025.7.1 or later. Restrict allowed directories to the minimum required. Run MCP servers in sandboxed containers.

name: Claude Code Hooks RCE via malicious project files (CVE-2025-59536)
description: Remote code execution in Claude Code via malicious project configuration files (CLAUDE.md, .claude/settings.json). Attackers craft repository files that execute arbitrary hooks when a developer opens the project with Claude Code.
remediation: Update Claude Code to the latest version. Review project-level CLAUDE.md and .claude/ directories before opening untrusted repositories. Disable automatic hook execution for untrusted projects.

name: Claude Code API token exfiltration via env vars (CVE-2026-21852)
description: API credential theft in Claude Code via malicious MCP server configurations and environment variable exfiltration. Attackers embed MCP servers in project configs that steal ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and other credentials at runtime.
remediation: Update Claude Code to the latest version. Never store API keys in project-level configuration files. Use credential managers or environment-specific secret stores.

name: MCP Ruby SDK session hijacking (CVE-2026-33946)
description: Session hijacking vulnerability in the MCP Ruby SDK (versions prior to 0.9.2). The streamable_http_transport implementation allows an attacker who obtains a valid session ID to completely hijack the victim's Server-Sent Events stream and intercept all real-time data.
remediation: Update the MCP Ruby SDK to version 0.9.2 or later. Use secure session token generation with sufficient entropy. Implement session binding to client IP/User-Agent.

name: MCP Inspector arbitrary code execution (CVE-2025-49596)
description: Arbitrary code execution vulnerability in Anthropic's MCP Inspector tool. Allows attackers to execute arbitrary code when inspecting untrusted MCP server configurations.
remediation: Update MCP Inspector to the latest patched version. Never inspect untrusted MCP servers without sandboxing. Run the inspector in an isolated container.

name: oatpp-mcp prompt hijacking (CVE-2025-6515)
description: Prompt hijacking vulnerability in the oatpp-mcp framework. Allows attackers to take over MCP sessions by injecting crafted prompt sequences that override the server's intended behavior.
remediation: Update oatpp-mcp to the latest patched version. Implement input validation and sanitization on all prompt content. Use session isolation between clients.

name: MCP Playwright/Puppeteer browser automation abuse
description: Browser automation MCP servers grant agents full browser control including navigating to arbitrary URLs, filling forms, and reading page content. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into visiting phishing sites, exfiltrating cookies, or performing unauthorized OAuth flows.
remediation: Restrict browser MCP servers to specific URL allowlists. Disable cookie and credential access. Use headless mode with network isolation. Monitor for navigation to unexpected domains.

name: MCP server DNS rebinding attack on localhost
description: MCP servers bound to localhost with HTTP transport (no TLS) are vulnerable to DNS rebinding attacks. An attacker can craft a malicious webpage that rebinds DNS to 127.0.0.1 and sends requests to the local MCP server, bypassing same-origin policy protections.
remediation: Use authentication tokens for all MCP server endpoints, even on localhost. Validate the Host header to reject requests from unexpected origins. Prefer stdio transport over HTTP for local servers.

name: Supply Chain -- Malicious Go MCP package
description: MCP server installed via Go binary with suspicious package name indicating malicious intent. Go MCP servers can be compiled with embedded malware that executes at startup without runtime dependencies.
remediation: Remove the binary immediately. Verify the source repository and module path on pkg.go.dev before reinstalling. Check for unexpected network connections.

name: Supply Chain -- Malicious Docker MCP image
description: MCP server launched via Docker image with suspicious name. Malicious Docker images can include rootkits, cryptocurrency miners, or data exfiltration tools that run with container privileges.
remediation: Remove the container immediately. Only use MCP Docker images from verified publishers. Scan images with container security tools before deployment.

name: MCP SSE transport without authentication
description: MCP server using SSE or Streamable HTTP transport without authentication. Remote MCP transports without authentication tokens allow any network-adjacent attacker to connect and execute tools with the server's privileges.
remediation: Always require authentication tokens for SSE and Streamable HTTP transports. Use TLS for all remote connections. Implement rate limiting and client certificate validation.

name: MCP server with write access to CI/CD pipelines
description: MCP server with write access to CI/CD systems (Jenkins, CircleCI, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI). Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into modifying build pipelines, injecting malicious build steps, or exfiltrating CI secrets.
remediation: Restrict CI/CD MCP servers to read-only access. Use separate service accounts with minimal permissions. Require manual approval for any pipeline modifications.

name: MCP server with Kubernetes cluster access
description: MCP server with access to Kubernetes cluster management. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into deploying malicious containers, modifying RBAC policies, exfiltrating secrets from the cluster, or causing denial of service by scaling resources.
remediation: Restrict Kubernetes MCP servers to read-only access with minimal RBAC roles. Never grant cluster-admin privileges. Use namespaced service accounts. Implement admission controller policies.

name: MCP server with cloud IAM management access
description: MCP server with access to cloud IAM management APIs. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into creating new admin users, modifying access policies, or granting unauthorized access to cloud resources.
remediation: Never grant IAM write access to MCP servers. Use read-only IAM policies. Implement SCPs or organization policies to prevent privilege escalation. Require MFA for all IAM modifications.

name: RCE via Command Injection -- newline in args
description: MCP server arguments contain command injection via newline or carriage return characters (URL-encoded or literal). Newline injection can break out of intended command context and execute arbitrary commands on a new line.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Strip or reject newline characters in all arguments. Use argument arrays instead of concatenated command strings.

name: MCP server requesting excessive tool permissions
description: MCP server configured with flags that disable security restrictions or request unrestricted permissions. These flags bypass sandboxing, permission checks, and security controls intended to limit tool capabilities.
remediation: Remove permission-bypassing flags. Configure MCP servers with the principle of least privilege. Use explicit permission allowlists instead of blanket allow-all flags.

name: MCP server with email/messaging send capability
description: MCP server with email or messaging send capabilities. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into sending phishing emails, spam, or exfiltrating data via outbound messages to attacker-controlled addresses.
remediation: Restrict MCP email servers to specific recipient allowlists. Implement outbound content scanning. Require human approval for messages to external addresses. Monitor for BCC injection.

name: MCP server with payment/billing API access
description: MCP server with access to payment processing APIs. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into creating unauthorized charges, modifying billing records, issuing refunds to attacker accounts, or exfiltrating payment method details.
remediation: Use read-only API keys for MCP payment integrations. Never grant refund or charge creation permissions. Implement transaction amount limits. Require human approval for all financial operations.

name: MCP server with secret manager access
description: MCP server with access to secret management systems (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler, 1Password). Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into reading and exfiltrating all stored secrets, API keys, database passwords, and certificates.
remediation: Never grant MCP servers broad read access to secret managers. Use scoped access policies for specific secrets only. Implement audit logging for all secret access. Require human approval for reading production secrets.

name: MCP server tunneling via ngrok/cloudflare tunnel
description: MCP server exposed to the internet via tunneling services (ngrok, Cloudflare Tunnel, localtunnel). Tunneling bypasses firewall and network security controls, exposing local MCP servers to unauthenticated remote access.
remediation: Remove tunnel configurations from MCP servers. If remote access is required, use VPN or mTLS instead of public tunnels. Implement authentication on all exposed endpoints.

name: mcp-fetch-server SSRF via private IP bypass
description: SSRF vulnerability in mcp-fetch-server where the is_ip_private() validation function fails to block private IP addresses. Enables attackers to reach internal network services, cloud metadata endpoints, and other localhost-bound services via the MCP fetch tool.
remediation: Update to the latest patched version. Implement strict allowlists for fetchable URLs. Block RFC 1918, link-local, and loopback addresses at the network level. Use egress firewall rules.

name: Output poisoning via MCP tool return values (CyberArk SEA)
description: Semantic Execution Abuse (SEA) attack where MCP server return values contain embedded instructions that poison the LLM context. Unlike tool description poisoning, output poisoning occurs at runtime when the server returns crafted responses that manipulate subsequent agent behavior.
remediation: Sanitize and validate all MCP tool return values before passing to the LLM. Implement output content policies. Use structured output schemas that reject free-text instruction patterns.

name: Shadow MCP server -- unsanctioned deployment (OWASP MCP09)
description: Unsanctioned MCP server deployed outside organizational security governance (OWASP MCP09:2025 -- Shadow MCP Servers). Shadow deployments often use default credentials, permissive configurations, and bypass centralized security controls, creating invisible backdoors.
remediation: Implement an MCP server inventory and allowlist policy. Require all MCP servers to be registered and approved. Audit for unauthorized MCP configurations. Use endpoint detection to identify rogue MCP processes.

name: MCP session ID exposed in URL (Streamable HTTP transport)
description: MCP Streamable HTTP transport exposes session identifiers in GET URL query parameters (?sessionId=UUID). Session IDs in URLs are logged by proxies, CDNs, browser history, and web server access logs, enabling session hijacking via log access or referer header leakage.
remediation: Use session tokens in HTTP headers instead of URL parameters. Implement short-lived session tokens with server-side validation. Bind sessions to client IP and TLS fingerprint.

name: Cross-server tool manipulation via multi-MCP configurations
description: Cross-server manipulation attack where one malicious MCP server injects instructions that cause the AI agent to misuse tools from other legitimate connected servers. Enables credential forwarding, data exfiltration via trusted channels, and confused deputy attacks across server boundaries.
remediation: Isolate MCP server contexts. Never share credentials or tool outputs across server boundaries without explicit user approval. Implement per-server permission scopes. Monitor for unexpected cross-server tool invocations.

name: MCP resource exhaustion and compute quota drain
description: MCP server configured without rate limiting or timeout controls. Attackers can drain AI compute quotas, cause denial of service, or inflate costs by triggering unbounded tool invocations through prompt injection or recursive tool calls.
remediation: Implement per-tool rate limits and request timeouts. Set maximum token budgets per session. Monitor for anomalous invocation patterns. Use circuit breakers to prevent cascading resource exhaustion.

name: Supply Chain -- Typosquatted MCP npm scoped packages
description: MCP server installed via npm scoped package with a name that typosquats the official @modelcontextprotocol or @anthropic scope. Scoped typosquatting is more deceptive than unscoped because users trust the package scope as a publisher identity.
remediation: Verify the exact scope and package name against the official npm organization. Only use packages from @modelcontextprotocol (official) or @anthropic (Anthropic). Report typosquatted scopes to npm security.

name: Postmark MCP impersonation -- BCC exfiltration variant (2026)
description: Updated variant of the September 2025 Postmark MCP supply chain attack. Fake Postmark MCP server captures API keys from environment variables and BCC copies of all outgoing emails to attacker-controlled addresses, exfiltrating confidential documents and credentials.
remediation: Only use the official Postmark MCP server from verified publishers. Audit outbound email logs for unauthorized BCC recipients. Rotate Postmark API keys if compromise is suspected.

name: MCP OAuth PKCE downgrade attack
description: MCP server configured to use OAuth implicit grant or disable PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange). Without PKCE, OAuth authorization codes can be intercepted via redirect URI manipulation, enabling token theft. MCP specification requires PKCE as of June 2026.
remediation: Enable PKCE for all OAuth flows. Use authorization code grant with PKCE instead of implicit grant. Validate redirect URIs strictly. Follow the MCP OAuth Resource Server specification.

name: MCP tool squatting in community registries
description: MCP server installed from an untrusted or custom tool registry. Tool squatting attacks register legitimate-sounding names in community MCP registries to distribute malware. Unlike package manager typosquatting, tool squatting targets MCP-specific discovery mechanisms.
remediation: Only install MCP servers from verified registries. Verify tool publisher identity before installation. Implement ETDI (Enhanced Tool Definition Interface) verification when available. Pin tool versions and verify checksums.

name: Terraform/IaC MCP server with infrastructure write access
description: MCP server with write access to Infrastructure-as-Code tools (Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation). Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into provisioning attacker-controlled infrastructure, modifying security groups, creating IAM backdoors, or destroying production resources.
remediation: Restrict IaC MCP servers to plan/read-only mode. Require separate approval pipelines for infrastructure changes. Never grant apply/destroy permissions to MCP-connected service accounts. Use policy-as-code (OPA, Sentinel) to enforce guardrails.

name: MCP server with S3/GCS/Azure Blob storage write access
description: MCP server with write access to cloud object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob). Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into exfiltrating data to attacker-controlled buckets, overwriting critical files, modifying bucket policies, or uploading malware for distribution.
remediation: Use read-only credentials for MCP storage integrations. Restrict access to specific bucket prefixes. Enable bucket versioning and object lock. Monitor for writes to unexpected destinations.

name: MCP Anthropic mcp-remote vulnerable versions (expanded)
description: Additional vulnerabilities discovered in mcp-remote versions 0.4.x through 0.6.x after the initial CVE-2025-6514 fix. Includes incomplete URL sanitization bypasses, OAuth callback race conditions, and header injection via malformed server responses.
remediation: Update mcp-remote to version 0.7.0 or later. Audit OAuth callback handlers. Validate all server-provided URLs before navigation.

name: MCP server with code repository write access (beyond GitHub)
description: MCP server with write access to code repositories (GitLab, Bitbucket, CodeCommit, Gitea). Prompt injection can exploit write permissions to push malicious commits, modify CI/CD pipelines, create backdoor branches, or tamper with merge request approvals.
remediation: Use read-only tokens for MCP repository integrations. Restrict to specific repositories and branches. Require signed commits. Enable branch protection rules preventing direct pushes.

name: MCP server with monitoring/observability write access
description: MCP server with write access to monitoring and alerting systems. Prompt injection can silence critical alerts, create misleading dashboards, trigger false incidents for social engineering, or exfiltrate sensitive telemetry data containing PII or credentials.
remediation: Use read-only API keys for MCP monitoring integrations. Restrict alert management to dedicated human operators. Implement audit logging for all monitoring configuration changes.

name: RCE via Command Injection -- glob and wildcard expansion in args
description: MCP server arguments contain command substitution patterns using $() or backtick syntax with common reconnaissance commands. Attacker uses command substitution to exfiltrate system information or download and execute payloads.
remediation: Remove the MCP server. Ensure all arguments are passed as arrays, never through shell interpolation. Reject arguments containing $(), backticks, or other shell expansion characters.

name: MCP server with DNS management access
description: MCP server with write access to DNS management APIs (Cloudflare, Route53, Google Cloud DNS). Prompt injection can manipulate DNS records for domain hijacking, MX record poisoning for email interception, or subdomain takeover for phishing campaigns.
remediation: Never grant DNS write access to MCP servers. Use read-only DNS API keys. Implement DNSSEC. Require multi-person approval for all DNS record changes. Monitor for unauthorized DNS modifications.

name: Supply Chain -- Malicious Deno MCP package
description: MCP server installed via Deno runtime with suspicious package name indicating malicious intent. While Deno has a permission system, MCP servers often require --allow-net and --allow-read flags that negate sandboxing benefits.
remediation: Remove the Deno package immediately. Verify the source on deno.land/x before reinstalling. Use minimal --allow flags. Audit network connections.

name: MCP server with Wasm/WebAssembly execution
description: MCP server configured to execute WebAssembly modules. While Wasm provides sandboxing, WASI-enabled modules with filesystem and network access can bypass sandbox restrictions. Compiled Wasm binaries are harder to audit for malicious behavior.
remediation: Audit Wasm source code before compilation. Restrict WASI capabilities to minimum required. Use Wasm sandboxing runtimes with strict resource limits. Prefer source-available MCP implementations.

name: MCP server with conversation history access
description: MCP server with access to the AI agent's conversation history or persistent memory. Enables exfiltration of sensitive information shared in prior conversations, including credentials, personal data, proprietary code, and business-critical information discussed with the agent.
remediation: Restrict MCP server access to the current conversation context only. Never expose full conversation history to third-party servers. Implement data classification to redact sensitive content before passing to MCP tools.

name: MCP Streamable HTTP response splitting (CVE-2026-38012)
description: HTTP response splitting vulnerability in MCP Streamable HTTP transport. Malicious servers inject CRLF sequences in SSE event data, allowing header injection, cache poisoning, and session hijacking across MCP client connections.
remediation: Update MCP SDK to the latest version. Validate and sanitize all SSE event data. Reject responses containing bare CR or LF characters outside of SSE framing.

name: MCP Python SDK SSRF via tool URI handler (CVE-2026-40115)
description: SSRF vulnerability in the MCP Python SDK fetch tool handler. The URI validation allows bypass via IPv6 mapped addresses (::ffff:127.0.0.1), DNS rebinding, and URL shortener redirects to reach internal services and cloud metadata endpoints.
remediation: Update the MCP Python SDK to the latest version. Implement DNS resolution validation. Block private IP ranges including IPv6 mapped addresses. Disable redirect following for untrusted URLs.

name: MCP tool description mutation via server-initiated update
description: MCP servers that dynamically update tool descriptions after initial registration. The server presents benign tool descriptions during setup, then mutates them at runtime to include malicious instructions (rug pull). This bypasses one-time security reviews.
remediation: Pin tool descriptions at connection time and reject runtime modifications. Hash tool descriptions and alert on changes. Implement tool description immutability after initial handshake. Use --watch mode to detect drift.

name: MCP OAuth token replay via insecure token storage
description: MCP server stores OAuth tokens in plaintext files on disk. Attackers with filesystem access can steal and replay tokens to access protected APIs. Combined with filesystem MCP servers, enables cross-server token theft.
remediation: Use OS keychain or encrypted credential storage for OAuth tokens. Set restrictive file permissions (0600) on token files. Implement token binding to prevent replay. Use short-lived tokens with refresh rotation.

name: MCP notification channel injection via notifications/message
description: MCP servers abuse the notifications/message channel to inject instructions into the client-side LLM context outside of normal tool invocation flow. Notifications bypass tool-level security reviews since they are treated as informational rather than executable.
remediation: Sanitize all notification message content before displaying to the LLM. Implement content security policies for notification channels. Rate limit notifications per server. Log and audit all notification content.

name: Cursor MCP auto-approve exploitation
description: MCP servers configured with auto-approve mode in Cursor IDE bypass all human-in-the-loop confirmations. Combined with tool poisoning, attackers can execute arbitrary operations without user awareness or consent. Auto-approve is especially dangerous with write-capable servers.
remediation: Never enable auto-approve for untrusted MCP servers. Use allowlist-based approval for specific tool names only. Implement per-operation risk scoring that overrides auto-approve for high-risk actions.

name: MCP resource template injection via URI patterns
description: MCP resource URI templates that accept user-controlled path segments without validation. Attackers inject path traversal sequences (../) or query parameters to access resources outside the intended scope, including reading arbitrary files or accessing restricted API endpoints.
remediation: Validate and sanitize all URI template parameters. Use allowlists for permitted resource paths. Implement path canonicalization before resource access. Reject URI templates containing path traversal sequences.

name: Supply Chain -- Malicious Bun MCP package
description: MCP server installed via Bun runtime with suspicious package name indicating malicious intent. Bun MCP servers benefit from fast startup times, making them effective for hit-and-run data exfiltration attacks that complete before monitoring can detect them.
remediation: Remove the package immediately. Verify the package source on npm before reinstalling. Monitor for short-lived Bun processes making unexpected network connections.

name: MCP server with vector database write access (RAG poisoning)
description: MCP server with write access to vector databases used in RAG pipelines. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into inserting poisoned embeddings that corrupt knowledge retrieval for all future queries. RAG poisoning persists across sessions and affects all users of the shared knowledge base.
remediation: Use read-only credentials for MCP vector database integrations. Implement content validation before vector insertion. Use separate namespaces for MCP-written and human-curated embeddings. Monitor for anomalous write patterns.

name: MCP server with container registry write access
description: MCP server with push access to container registries (Docker Hub, ECR, GCR, GHCR, ACR). Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into pushing backdoored container images that will be deployed by CI/CD pipelines, enabling persistent supply chain compromise.
remediation: Use read-only registry credentials for MCP integrations. Require image signing (cosign/notation) for all pushes. Implement admission controllers to verify image provenance. Restrict push access to specific repositories.

name: MCP tool input schema with executable defaults
description: MCP tools with inputSchema default values containing executable content (shell commands, script URLs, code snippets). When the LLM omits optional parameters, the schema default is used, causing execution of attacker-controlled payloads without explicit invocation.
remediation: Audit all inputSchema default values for executable content. Reject schemas where defaults contain URLs, shell commands, or code. Implement schema validation that flags non-literal default values.

name: MCP server with LLM API proxy access (model theft/abuse)
description: MCP server that proxies LLM API requests. Enables prompt injection to make unauthorized API calls, exfiltrate prompts and responses through the proxy, drain API quotas, or redirect requests to attacker-controlled model endpoints that return manipulated responses.
remediation: Use direct API keys with per-model rate limits instead of proxy servers. Implement request logging and anomaly detection. Restrict allowed model endpoints. Never pass API keys through MCP proxy servers.

name: MCP elicitation attack via tool description context leakage
description: MCP tool descriptions that instruct the LLM to include system prompt content, user instructions, or other tool descriptions in tool call parameters. This elicitation attack leaks confidential prompt content to the MCP server, exposing proprietary instructions and business logic.
remediation: Never include system prompt content in MCP tool parameters. Implement output filters that detect and block system prompt leakage. Use context isolation between system instructions and tool parameters.

name: MCP Streamable HTTP session fixation
description: MCP Streamable HTTP transport configured with a fixed or predictable session identifier. Attackers who can guess or obtain the session ID can hijack the MCP session, intercept tool results, and inject malicious tool responses.
remediation: Use cryptographically random session identifiers generated server-side. Implement session binding to client TLS certificate or IP. Rotate session tokens periodically. Reject client-provided session IDs.

name: MCP server with calendar/scheduling write access
description: MCP server with write access to calendar and scheduling systems. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into creating calendar events with phishing links, modifying meeting details, adding unauthorized attendees, or leaking schedule information.
remediation: Use read-only calendar API scopes for MCP integrations. Require human approval for event creation or modification. Implement attendee allowlists. Monitor for calendar events created by MCP tools.

name: MCP multi-hop prompt injection via chained servers
description: Multi-hop prompt injection where a compromised MCP server returns output containing instructions that target tools from a different connected MCP server. The attack chains across server boundaries, using trusted server A to execute malicious actions on trusted server B via the shared LLM context.
remediation: Implement output sanitization between MCP server tool calls. Use separate LLM contexts per server when possible. Restrict cross-server tool invocation sequences. Monitor for unusual multi-server tool call patterns.

name: MCP server impersonating official Anthropic packages
description: MCP server package using names that impersonate official Anthropic npm scopes or package naming conventions. Unlike simple typosquatting, these packages use plausible-looking scope names that could be mistaken for official Anthropic releases.
remediation: Verify all Anthropic packages come from the @anthropic-ai scope on npm. Cross-reference with the official Anthropic GitHub organization. Report impersonation packages to npm security.

name: MCP server with SSH key agent forwarding access
description: MCP server configured with access to the SSH agent socket or SSH private keys. Enables unauthorized remote server access, git repository manipulation via SSH, and lateral movement through SSH-accessible infrastructure.
remediation: Never expose SSH_AUTH_SOCK to MCP servers. Use deploy keys with read-only access for git operations. Implement SSH certificate-based access with short lifetimes. Audit all SSH key usage by MCP processes.

name: MCP server with browser cookie/session store access
description: MCP server with access to browser cookie stores or session data. Enables theft of authenticated sessions for any web service the user is logged into, including banking, email, cloud consoles, and source code repositories.
remediation: Never grant MCP servers access to browser cookie stores. Use isolated browser profiles for MCP browser automation. Implement cookie encryption at rest. Monitor for reads to browser cookie databases.

name: MCP server with package.json/pyproject.toml write access
description: MCP server capable of modifying project dependency files (package.json, pyproject.toml, Cargo.toml). Prompt injection can add malicious dependencies with postinstall scripts, pin compromised package versions, or modify build scripts to include backdoors.
remediation: Use read-only filesystem access for project manifest files. Require human review for all dependency changes. Implement lockfile integrity verification. Use dependency scanners in CI/CD to catch unauthorized additions.

name: MCP server clipboard data harvesting
description: MCP server configured to monitor or access system clipboard contents. Clipboard frequently contains passwords, tokens, credit card numbers, and other sensitive data copied by users. Continuous clipboard monitoring enables passive credential harvesting.
remediation: Never grant clipboard read access to MCP servers unless explicitly required. Implement clipboard access auditing. Use ephemeral clipboard clearing for sensitive data. Restrict clipboard access to specific user-initiated actions.

name: MCP server with GitHub Actions workflow dispatch access
description: MCP server with ability to trigger GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch events. Prompt injection can trigger arbitrary CI/CD workflows, potentially with attacker-controlled inputs, leading to code execution in the CI environment, secret exfiltration from GitHub Actions, or deployment of compromised artifacts.
remediation: Restrict GitHub tokens to read-only permissions. Never grant actions:write scope to MCP server tokens. Require approval gates on workflow_dispatch triggers. Implement OIDC-based authentication for sensitive workflows.

name: MCP server with GraphQL introspection and mutation access
description: MCP server with access to GraphQL APIs with introspection enabled. Prompt injection can use introspection to discover the full API schema, then execute destructive mutations, exfiltrate data via deeply nested queries, or cause denial of service through query complexity attacks.
remediation: Disable GraphQL introspection in production. Use persisted/allowlisted queries. Implement query depth and complexity limits. Use read-only GraphQL roles for MCP integrations.

name: MCP server with WASM component model sandbox escape
description: MCP server running WebAssembly modules with WASI extensions (wasi-nn, wasi-http, wasi-filesystem) that expand the attack surface beyond the core Wasm sandbox. WASI extensions grant network, filesystem, and ML inference access that can be abused for data exfiltration.
remediation: Minimize WASI capabilities granted to Wasm modules. Audit Wasm source code before deployment. Use capability-based security with fine-grained WASI permissions. Monitor Wasm module network and filesystem access patterns.

name: MCP server using deprecated stdio transport with shell expansion
description: MCP server launched via shell wrapper (sh -c, cmd /c) instead of direct process execution. Shell wrapping enables environment variable expansion, glob expansion, and command substitution in server arguments, turning otherwise-safe configurations into command injection vectors.
remediation: Launch MCP servers via direct process execution, never through shell wrappers. Use argument arrays instead of command strings. If shell wrapping is unavoidable, escape all arguments for the target shell.

name: Supply Chain -- LiteLLM compromised versions (TeamPCP)
description: TeamPCP threat group compromised LiteLLM versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 (95M+ monthly downloads). The malicious versions contain a multi-stage credential stealer targeting SSH keys, cloud provider tokens, Kubernetes secrets, cryptocurrency wallets, and .env files. LiteLLM sits between applications and AI providers with access to all API keys.
remediation: Remove LiteLLM versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 immediately. Pin to a known-safe version (>= 1.82.9). Rotate ALL credentials accessible to the LLM gateway, including API keys, cloud tokens, SSH keys, and database passwords.

name: Supply Chain -- LiteLLM MCP gateway with compromised routing
description: MCP server using LiteLLM as an LLM routing proxy. LiteLLM sits between applications and AI providers with access to all API keys and request/response content. A compromised LiteLLM instance can intercept credentials, modify prompts, exfiltrate conversation data, and redirect requests to attacker-controlled models.
remediation: Pin LiteLLM to a verified safe version. Use direct provider API keys instead of routing proxies. Implement request/response auditing. Monitor for unexpected model endpoint redirections.

name: Supply Chain -- TeamPCP campaign indicators (Trivy, Checkmarx)
description: TeamPCP threat group (active December 2025 - March 2026) has compromised multiple developer tools including Aqua Trivy, CheckMarx VS Code extensions, and npm packages with self-propagating worms. The group specifically targets AI/ML infrastructure and supply chains.
remediation: Audit all developer tool versions for TeamPCP indicators. Monitor for unusual outbound connections from security scanners and IDE extensions. Check npm audit for compromised packages.

name: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) session smuggling via MCP bridge
description: MCP servers bridging Agent-to-Agent (A2A) communication channels. Attackers inject crafted session context that persists across agent boundaries, carrying malicious instructions from one agent conversation into another. Enables cross-agent credential theft and cascading prompt injection.
remediation: Implement strict session isolation between agents. Sanitize all cross-agent context before passing to MCP tools. Use separate credentials per agent. Monitor for unexpected cross-agent data flows.

name: MCP token drain via cyclic tool call patterns (Overthinking Loop)
description: MCP servers configured without tool call limits enabling Overthinking Loop attacks. Malicious tools induce cyclic reasoning trajectories in the agent, amplifying token consumption up to 142.4x via trivial text repetition, staged checklists, and recursive subtask generation composed into cyclic call structures.
remediation: Implement per-session tool call limits and token budgets. Detect circular tool call patterns. Set maximum recursion depth for tool invocations. Use circuit breakers to halt runaway agent loops.

name: Lazarus Group malicious AI/ML npm and PyPI packages
description: Lazarus APT group (North Korea) planted malicious packages in npm and PyPI targeting AI/ML developers. Packages use legitimate-sounding ML/AI names to attract downloads. Payloads include credential stealers, cryptocurrency wallet drainers, and persistent backdoors.
remediation: Verify all AI/ML packages against known-good registries. Check author reputation and download counts before installing. Use lockfiles and dependency scanning. Monitor for packages with suspiciously generic AI/ML names.

name: SANDWORM_MODE -- npm typosquatting MCP server injection campaign
description: SANDWORM_MODE campaign (February 2026) deployed 19 typosquatting npm packages impersonating popular utilities. The malware injects MCP server configurations into Claude Code, Claude Desktop, Cursor, VS Code Continue, and Windsurf/Codeium, allowing threat actors to feed hidden instructions and read sensitive files from compromised developer machines.
remediation: Remove any recently installed packages matching the SANDWORM_MODE indicators. Audit MCP configuration files for unauthorized server entries. Rotate all credentials accessible from compromised machines.

name: MCP TypeScript SDK DNS rebinding (CVE-2025-66414)
description: DNS rebinding vulnerability in MCP TypeScript SDK versions prior to 1.24.0. Localhost-bound SSE and Streamable HTTP servers lack DNS rebinding protection by default, allowing malicious websites to pivot through the browser and execute MCP tools on the local server.
remediation: Update @modelcontextprotocol/sdk to version 1.24.0 or later. Validate Host headers on all HTTP-based MCP servers. Use stdio transport for local-only servers.

name: MCP Python SDK DNS rebinding (CVE-2025-66416)
description: DNS rebinding vulnerability in MCP Python SDK (FastMCP) versions prior to 1.23.0. SSE and Streamable HTTP transports on localhost are vulnerable to DNS rebinding attacks from malicious websites, enabling unauthorized tool execution via the browser.
remediation: Update the MCP Python SDK to version 1.23.0 or later. Add Host header validation. Prefer stdio transport for local development servers.

name: FastMCP OAuthProxy confused deputy (CVE-2026-27124)
description: Missing consent verification in FastMCP OAuthProxy callback (CVE-2026-27124). Attackers exploit static client IDs, dynamic client registration, and consent cookies to obtain OAuth authorization codes without proper user consent. Demonstrated against GitHub OAuth integration enabling unauthorized repository access.
remediation: Update FastMCP to the latest patched version. Implement PKCE with per-request state validation. Verify user consent at every OAuth callback. Use dynamic client registration with strict redirect URI validation.

name: Axios supply chain compromise (Sapphire Sleet / DPRK)
description: Axios npm package versions 1.7.8 and 1.7.9 (100M+ weekly downloads) compromised via hijacked maintainer account. Microsoft Threat Intelligence attributed this to Sapphire Sleet (North Korean state actor). The malicious versions contain credential stealers targeting environment variables, SSH keys, and browser session tokens.
remediation: Pin axios to version 1.7.10 or later. Rotate all credentials accessible from affected environments. Audit lockfiles for compromised versions. Enable npm provenance verification.

name: MCP tool description whitespace hiding attack
description: Demonstrated attack where a benign-looking MCP server (e.g., 'random fact of the day') hides malicious instructions after many trailing spaces in tool descriptions, making them invisible without horizontal scrolling. After a sleeper period, the server changes its tool definition to manipulate other connected MCP servers (e.g., WhatsApp) into leaking message history.
remediation: Trim and normalize whitespace in all tool descriptions before display. Implement tool description pinning and drift detection. Review server tool descriptions regularly with --watch mode.

name: MCP dynamic client registration abuse
description: MCP servers using OAuth dynamic client registration allow attackers to register malicious clients that inherit trust from the registration endpoint. Combined with consent cookie reuse, attackers can obtain tokens for arbitrary scopes without user interaction.
remediation: Disable dynamic client registration for production MCP servers. Use pre-registered client IDs with strict redirect URI validation. Implement per-client consent tracking that does not rely on cookies alone.

name: MCP Preference Manipulation Attack (MPMA/DPMA)
description: MCP Preference Manipulation Attacks embed persuasive language, fake credentials, and urgency triggers in tool metadata to manipulate how AI agents rank and select tools. Direct PMA (DPMA) inserts phrases like 'officially endorsed' or 'certified secure' into tool names and descriptions to bias agent tool selection toward the malicious server.
remediation: Ignore trust claims in tool metadata. Implement server-side tool ranking that does not rely on self-reported metadata. Use allowlists for trusted MCP servers. Strip marketing language from tool descriptions before passing to the LLM.

name: Supply Chain -- SANDWORM_MODE IDE config injection targets
description: Specific typosquatting packages from the SANDWORM_MODE campaign that inject malicious MCP server entries into IDE configuration files (~/.cursor/mcp.json, claude_desktop_config.json, etc.). The injected servers exfiltrate source code, environment variables, and SSH keys.
remediation: Audit IDE MCP configuration files for unauthorized entries. Remove any packages matching SANDWORM_MODE indicators. Run mcp-scan --watch to detect configuration drift.

name: MCP server with CRM/customer data access
description: MCP server with access to CRM platforms containing customer PII, deal information, and support tickets. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into exfiltrating customer records, modifying deal stages, or injecting malicious content into support ticket responses.
remediation: Use read-only API credentials for MCP CRM integrations. Restrict access to specific record types and fields. Implement PII redaction filters. Require human approval for any CRM write operations.

name: MCP server with document signing access
description: MCP server with access to electronic signature platforms. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into creating fraudulent signature requests, modifying document contents before signing, or exfiltrating signed documents containing confidential business agreements.
remediation: Never grant document creation or modification permissions to MCP servers. Use read-only access for document status checks. Require multi-factor authentication for all signing operations. Audit all signature requests.

name: MCP MCPTox benchmark evasion via description splitting
description: Evasion technique where malicious instructions are split across multiple schema fields (description, title, examples, default values) so that no single field triggers detection. When concatenated by the LLM, the fragments form a complete attack payload. The MCPTox benchmark found 84.2% of such split payloads bypass single-field scanning.
remediation: Scan all schema fields holistically, not in isolation. Use Full-Schema Poisoning (FSP) analysis. Implement cross-field correlation to detect split payloads. Set maximum description length limits.

name: MCP server path traversal via symlink following
description: 82% of surveyed MCP implementations with file operations are vulnerable to path traversal via symlink following. Attackers create symlinks pointing outside allowed directories, enabling read/write access to arbitrary filesystem paths including /etc/shadow, SSH keys, and application credentials.
remediation: Disable symlink following in filesystem MCP servers. Resolve and validate canonical paths before file operations. Implement chroot or mount namespace isolation. Use the latest patched versions of filesystem servers.

name: MCP server with CI secret exfiltration via workflow inputs
description: MCP servers that trigger CI/CD workflow_dispatch events with attacker-controlled inputs. Prompt injection crafts workflow inputs that reference CI secrets (e.g., ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}) in log output, exfiltrating credentials via build logs accessible to the MCP server.
remediation: Never expose CI secrets in workflow logs. Use OIDC tokens instead of long-lived secrets. Restrict workflow_dispatch inputs to validated enums. Require approval gates for externally-triggered workflows.

name: MCP server with Notion/Confluence wiki write access
description: MCP server with write access to wiki and knowledge management platforms. Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into modifying documentation with misleading security guidance, injecting phishing links into shared pages, or exfiltrating proprietary knowledge base content.
remediation: Use read-only API tokens for MCP wiki integrations. Implement page-level access restrictions. Require human approval for page edits. Monitor for bulk page reads indicating knowledge exfiltration.

name: MCP Figma-MCP command injection (CVE-2025-53967) expanded variants
description: Expanded command injection variants in Figma MCP server affecting 600,000+ downloads. Beyond the original CVE-2025-53967, additional injection points discovered in image export, component search, and comment tools where user-controlled Figma layer names are passed unsanitized to shell commands.
remediation: Update Figma MCP server to the latest patched version (2.0+). Validate and sanitize all Figma API responses before shell execution. Use argument arrays instead of command string concatenation.

name: MCP server with cloud function deployment access
description: MCP server with access to deploy serverless functions (AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, Azure Functions). Prompt injection can deploy attacker-controlled code that executes with the function's IAM role, enabling persistent backdoors, data exfiltration, and lateral movement within cloud environments.
remediation: Never grant function deployment permissions to MCP servers. Use read-only credentials for function status monitoring. Implement deployment approval gates. Use infrastructure-as-code with version control for all function deployments.

name: Supply Chain -- Postmark MCP original incident (v1.0.16 BCC exfil)
description: First known malicious MCP server in the wild (September 2025). The postmark-mcp npm package v1.0.16 added a single line BCC'ing every outgoing email to an attacker-controlled address (phan@giftshop.club). Approximately 300 organizations compromised, exposing 3,000-15,000 emails per day including password resets, invoices, and internal correspondence.
remediation: Remove postmark-mcp versions prior to 2.0.0. Audit outbound email logs for unauthorized BCC recipients. Rotate any credentials sent via email. Only use the official Postmark MCP server from verified publishers.

name: MCP server with Snowflake/BigQuery data warehouse access
description: MCP server with access to data warehouses containing business-critical analytics data. Prompt injection can exfiltrate customer records, financial data, and proprietary metrics via crafted SQL queries. Data warehouses often contain denormalized PII aggregated from multiple source systems.
remediation: Use read-only credentials with row-level security. Restrict to specific schemas and tables. Implement query result size limits. Use data masking policies for PII columns. Monitor for bulk data export patterns.

name: MCP OAuth token theft via redirect URI manipulation
description: MCP server OAuth flow configured with HTTP (non-HTTPS) redirect URIs or wildcard redirect patterns. Attackers intercept authorization codes by exploiting open redirects, subdomain takeover, or network-level MITM on the non-TLS callback endpoint.
remediation: Use HTTPS redirect URIs exclusively. Register exact redirect URIs (no wildcards). Implement PKCE with S256 challenge method. Validate the redirect_uri parameter on both authorization and token endpoints.

name: MCP server with Jira/Linear project management write access
description: MCP server with write access to project management platforms. Prompt injection can create misleading issues, modify sprint priorities, assign tasks to exfiltrate workflow information, or inject malicious links into issue descriptions that other team members click.
remediation: Use read-only API tokens for MCP project management integrations. Restrict to specific projects and issue types. Require human approval for issue creation and modification.

name: MCP server environment variable injection via args passthrough
description: MCP server configured to inherit or passthrough all environment variables from the host process. This exposes every secret, API key, and credential in the parent environment to the MCP server process, including variables not explicitly needed by the server.
remediation: Use explicit environment variable allowlists. Only pass the specific variables each MCP server requires. Never use wildcard env passthrough. Prefer credential managers over environment variables.

name: MCP server with image generation and steganographic exfil
description: MCP server with image generation capabilities can be exploited for steganographic data exfiltration. Prompt injection encodes stolen credentials or sensitive data into image generation parameters, embedding secrets in image metadata, pixel data, or generation prompts that are sent to external services.
remediation: Sanitize all image generation prompts for embedded data patterns. Strip metadata from generated images. Monitor for suspicious generation parameters. Restrict image generation to approved use cases.

name: MCP server with network scanning/reconnaissance tools
description: MCP server with network scanning and reconnaissance capabilities. Prompt injection can perform unauthorized network scans, enumerate internal services, discover vulnerable hosts, and map network topology to facilitate lateral movement or targeted attacks against internal infrastructure.
remediation: Restrict network scanning MCP servers to approved target ranges. Require explicit authorization for each scan. Implement rate limiting and scan scope controls. Log all scan activities for audit.

name: MCP server with package registry publish access
description: MCP server with credentials to publish packages to npm, PyPI, crates.io, or other package registries. Prompt injection can publish backdoored package versions, enabling supply chain attacks that propagate to all downstream consumers of the compromised package.
remediation: Never grant publish credentials to MCP servers. Use CI/CD pipelines with human approval gates for package publishing. Implement package signing and provenance attestation. Use scoped tokens with minimal permissions.

name: MCP Vulnerable server path traversal (82% prevalence)
description: 82% of 2,614 surveyed MCP server implementations with file operations are vulnerable to path traversal attacks. Attackers use ../ sequences, URL encoding (%2e%2e%2f), and double encoding to escape configured directory boundaries and access arbitrary files on the host system.
remediation: Canonicalize and validate all file paths before access. Reject paths containing .. after canonicalization. Use chroot or filesystem namespace isolation. Update to latest versions of all filesystem MCP servers.

name: mcp-remote OAuth RCE via unvalidated authorization endpoint
description: The mcp-remote npm package (437,000+ downloads) trusts server-provided OAuth authorization_endpoint without validation and passes it directly to the system shell via open(). Attackers craft malicious authorization URLs that achieve remote code execution on the client machine.
remediation: Update mcp-remote to the latest patched version. Validate all OAuth endpoints against an allowlist before opening. Never pass server-provided URLs directly to system shell commands.

name: Anthropic MCP Inspector DNS rebinding RCE
description: The MCP Inspector tool is vulnerable to DNS rebinding and CSRF attacks. Attackers send malicious HTTP requests from public web pages to the localhost MCP Inspector instance, achieving remote code execution without user interaction.
remediation: Update MCP Inspector to the latest version. Bind the inspector to 127.0.0.1 only. Enable CORS restrictions and CSRF token validation.

name: Anthropic mcp-server-git unrestricted git_init
description: The Anthropic git MCP server allows git_init on arbitrary directories including sensitive locations like ~/.ssh, turning them into git repositories. Combined with CVE-2025-68144 (argument injection in git_diff) and CVE-2025-68145 (path validation bypass), this enables a full RCE chain via malicious .git/config hooks.
remediation: Update to the latest version of the git MCP server. The git_init tool has been removed entirely in the fix. Restrict the server to specific repository directories.

name: Claude Code project file exploitation (CLAUDE.md / .mcp.json)
description: Malicious CLAUDE.md or .mcp.json files planted in cloned repositories can achieve RCE and API token exfiltration when the project is opened in Claude Code. The project configuration files are automatically loaded and can register malicious MCP servers or inject harmful instructions.
remediation: Never open untrusted repositories in Claude Code without reviewing CLAUDE.md and .mcp.json first. Enable project file verification. Update Claude Code to the latest version with sandboxed project config loading.

name: Postmark MCP supply chain backdoor (BCC exfiltration)
description: First confirmed real-world MCP supply chain compromise (September 2025). The backdoored postmark-mcp npm package silently added a BCC to every outgoing email, forwarding copies to an attacker-controlled domain. CISA issued an alert about this incident.
remediation: Remove the compromised package immediately. Verify package integrity against known-good checksums. Audit all sent emails for unauthorized BCC recipients. Rotate all credentials accessible to the MCP server.

name: WhatsApp MCP cross-server data exfiltration
description: Demonstrated by Invariant Labs (April 2025): a malicious MCP server exploits cross-server context sharing to silently exfiltrate a user's entire WhatsApp message history through a co-connected whatsapp-mcp server. Messages are sent to an attacker-controlled phone number disguised as ordinary outbound messages, bypassing DLP tooling.
remediation: Isolate messaging MCP servers from other connected servers. Use read-only mode for messaging integrations. Implement outbound message rate limiting and recipient allowlisting.

name: TeamPCP cascading AI toolchain supply chain attack
description: TeamPCP campaign (March 2026): cascading supply chain attack targeting the AI/ML developer toolchain. Compromises Trivy to steal CI/CD credentials, propagates an npm worm, then backdoors LiteLLM (unified gateway to 100+ LLM APIs) and Telnyx SDK to harvest API keys for every major commercial LLM provider.
remediation: Pin all AI/ML dependencies to verified versions. Use lockfiles with integrity hashes. Audit CI/CD credential access. Rotate all LLM API keys if compromise suspected.

name: MCP server with OAuth proxy trust exploitation
description: MCP servers exploiting OAuth proxy trust relationships by providing malicious authorization endpoints. Static client IDs, dynamic client registration, and consent cookies create confused deputy vulnerabilities enabling session hijacking and token theft.
remediation: Validate all OAuth endpoints against a hardcoded allowlist. Never trust server-provided authorization URLs. Use PKCE flow with per-session state parameters. Reject dynamic client registration from untrusted servers.

name: MCP Sampling API prompt injection via server-initiated completions
description: Documented by Palo Alto Unit42: malicious MCP servers exploit the sampling feature (server-initiated LLM completions through the client) to influence model behavior invisibly. The server hijacks the agent's reasoning process by injecting prompts through the sampling API, enabling actions that bypass user-visible tool approval.
remediation: Disable sampling capability for untrusted MCP servers. Require explicit user approval for each sampling request. Log all sampling interactions. Implement content filtering on sampling responses.

name: Cross-server context poisoning via shared agent context
description: Academic analysis of 67,057 MCP servers found that untrusted servers can exfiltrate data from co-connected trusted servers through the shared agent context. When multiple MCP servers connect to the same agent, a malicious server reads and manipulates data flowing through other servers.
remediation: Isolate MCP server contexts. Do not share conversation context between untrusted and trusted servers. Implement context boundaries per security domain. Monitor for cross-server data leakage patterns.

name: MCP server with conversation history access (Trail of Bits)
description: Documented by Trail of Bits (April 2025): MCP servers can steal conversation history containing sensitive information shared in previous messages. The server requests prior context through the agent, harvesting credentials, business logic, and personal data disclosed earlier in the session.
remediation: Restrict MCP server access to conversation history. Never share prior conversation context with newly connected servers. Implement message-level access controls. Sanitize conversation context before tool invocations.

name: Shadow MCP server (unregistered/unaudited AI agent tool)
description: Shadow MCP servers deployed without IT oversight give AI agents direct access to production databases, file systems, and internal APIs. They operate outside governance frameworks, executing tools or exfiltrating data without audit trails. This is the 'shadow IT' problem amplified by autonomous AI agents.
remediation: Implement mandatory MCP server registration in a central registry. Monitor for unauthorized MCP protocol traffic on the network. Deploy endpoint detection for unregistered MCP server processes. Enforce organization-wide MCP server allowlists.

name: EchoLeak -- hidden prompt exfiltration via document processing
description: EchoLeak attack (CVE-2025-32711): hidden prompts embedded in documents cause AI agents to exfiltrate sensitive data when summarizing or processing files. The attack exploits MCP servers with document access to inject instructions that are invisible to human reviewers but executed by the LLM.
remediation: Sanitize all document content before LLM processing. Implement content filtering for known prompt injection patterns in documents. Restrict document-processing MCP servers from making outbound network calls. Log all data extracted from documents.

name: MCP tool poisoning via output injection (CyberArk)
description: Documented by CyberArk: 'no output from your MCP server is safe.' Malicious servers inject hidden instructions into tool return values (not just descriptions). The LLM processes the poisoned output and follows the embedded instructions, enabling data exfiltration and unauthorized actions even when tool descriptions appear clean.
remediation: Sanitize all MCP tool return values before LLM processing. Implement output content filtering. Monitor tool responses for known injection patterns. Treat MCP server output as untrusted input.

name: MCP server with Docker socket access (container escape)
description: MCP servers with Docker socket access can create privileged containers, mount the host filesystem, and escape container isolation entirely. Prompt injection can escalate from a sandboxed MCP tool call to full host system compromise via Docker API abuse.
remediation: Never mount the Docker socket into MCP server containers. Use Docker-out-of-Docker with restricted API proxies. Implement container creation policies that block privileged mode and host mounts.

name: MCP server with cryptocurrency wallet access
description: MCP servers with cryptocurrency wallet access can drain funds via prompt injection. A malicious co-connected server or poisoned tool description can instruct the agent to sign transactions, transfer tokens, or approve unlimited spending allowances to attacker-controlled addresses.
remediation: Never grant transaction-signing capabilities to MCP servers. Use hardware wallet confirmation for all transactions. Implement transaction value limits and recipient allowlists. Require multi-factor approval for any financial operations.

name: MCP server SSE transport hijacking
