description: MCP servers using Server-Sent Events (SSE) transport without TLS or authentication are vulnerable to connection hijacking. Attackers on the same network can intercept the SSE stream, inject malicious tool responses, or replay captured sessions to execute unauthorized tool calls.
remediation: Always use TLS for SSE transport. Implement per-message authentication tokens. Use the newer Streamable HTTP transport with built-in security features. Validate server identity via certificate pinning.

name: MCP tool annotation spoofing (readOnlyHint / destructiveHint bypass)
description: MCP tool annotations (readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint, openWorldHint) are advisory-only and not enforced by the protocol. Malicious servers can mark destructive tools as read-only to bypass client safety checks, or mark safe tools as destructive to trigger unnecessary confirmation fatigue that users eventually dismiss.
remediation: Do not rely solely on tool annotations for security decisions. Implement server-side enforcement of tool capabilities. Verify tool behavior through sandboxed test execution. Cross-reference annotations with actual tool behavior analysis.

name: MCP resource template injection (arbitrary file/URL access)
description: MCP resource URI templates with insufficient validation allow attackers to craft URIs that access files outside intended boundaries or fetch content from arbitrary URLs. Template parameter injection (e.g., path traversal in template variables) bypasses directory restrictions.
remediation: Validate all resource URI template parameters. Canonicalize paths before access. Restrict URI schemes to explicit allowlists. Implement directory boundaries that cannot be bypassed via template variable injection.

name: Agentic worm propagation via connected MCP servers
description: Self-replicating malicious instructions that spread through connected MCP server ecosystems. Once a single agent is compromised via prompt injection, the payload instructs the agent to inject the same malicious instructions into every connected MCP server's tool descriptions or stored data, creating a worm that propagates across organizations.
remediation: Implement strict write controls on MCP server tool definitions. Monitor for unauthorized tool description modifications. Use content-addressable integrity verification for all tool metadata. Isolate MCP server networks to prevent cross-organization propagation.

name: MCP server with CI/CD pipeline access (supply chain amplification)
description: MCP servers with CI/CD pipeline access can be exploited via prompt injection to modify build scripts, inject backdoors into release artifacts, or exfiltrate CI/CD secrets. This amplifies a single agent compromise into a supply chain attack affecting all downstream consumers of the built software.
remediation: Use read-only CI/CD tokens for MCP integrations. Require human approval for any pipeline modifications. Implement signed commits and artifact attestation. Never grant MCP servers access to CI/CD secret stores.

name: MCP server with DNS/domain management access (domain takeover)
description: MCP servers with DNS management access can redirect domains to attacker-controlled infrastructure via prompt injection. A single poisoned tool call can modify DNS records to intercept all traffic for critical services, enabling credential harvesting, data exfiltration, and service impersonation at scale.
remediation: Never grant DNS write access to MCP servers. Use read-only DNS API tokens. Implement DNS change monitoring and alerting. Require multi-person approval for all DNS record modifications.

name: MCP server typosquatting on popular package registries
description: Typosquatted MCP server packages on npm, PyPI, and other registries mimic popular server names with subtle misspellings. Users installing MCP servers via quick npx/pip commands are especially vulnerable as package names are not verified against an official registry.
remediation: Verify exact package names against official MCP server registries before installation. Use lockfiles with integrity hashes. Implement package name verification in CI/CD. Report typosquatted packages to the registry.

name: MCP Elicitation API social engineering abuse
description: The MCP Elicitation feature allows servers to request additional information from users through the client. Malicious servers abuse this to social-engineer users into providing credentials, approving dangerous actions, or revealing sensitive information through carefully crafted elicitation prompts that mimic legitimate system dialogs.
remediation: Clearly label all elicitation prompts with the requesting server identity. Implement elicitation rate limiting. Never enter credentials into MCP elicitation dialogs. Restrict elicitation capabilities for untrusted servers.

name: MCP server exploiting Streamable HTTP transport for SSRF
description: MCP servers using the Streamable HTTP transport can be exploited for Server-Side Request Forgery. The transport endpoint can be pointed at internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), or localhost-bound services, enabling network reconnaissance and credential theft from internal infrastructure.
remediation: Validate all Streamable HTTP endpoint URLs against allowlists. Block connections to RFC 1918 addresses, link-local addresses, and cloud metadata endpoints. Implement network-level egress filtering for MCP server containers.

name: Axios npm supply chain compromise (RAT injection)
description: North Korean state actor (Sapphire Sleet) injected a Remote Access Trojan into Axios versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 via a malicious dependency 'plain-crypto-js' (March 31, 2026). MCP servers depending on affected Axios versions inherit the RAT, enabling remote control of the host system.
remediation: Pin Axios to versions before 1.14.1 or after the patched release. Audit node_modules for 'plain-crypto-js' dependency. Run npm audit and check for known compromised versions. Rotate all credentials on affected systems.

name: MCP server with memory/RAG write access (persistent context poisoning)
description: MCP servers with write access to agent memory, RAG stores, or vector databases can persistently corrupt the agent's knowledge base. Poisoned embeddings or stored instructions influence all future agent actions long after the initial injection, creating a durable persistence mechanism that survives session boundaries (OWASP ASI06).
remediation: Implement write-once semantics for memory stores. Require human approval for all memory writes. Maintain integrity hashes for stored knowledge. Implement memory versioning with rollback capability. Regularly audit stored embeddings for anomalous content.

name: MCP Streamable HTTP session fixation via response splitting
description: The Streamable HTTP transport (replacing SSE in MCP spec 2025-03-26) is vulnerable to HTTP response splitting when the Mcp-Session-Id header is not validated. Attackers inject CRLF sequences into session IDs to hijack sessions, poison response streams, or perform cache poisoning against downstream HTTP caches. This affects all MCP SDKs that implemented Streamable HTTP before April 2026 patches.
remediation: Update all MCP SDKs to latest versions. Validate Mcp-Session-Id headers against strict alphanumeric patterns. Reject any session IDs containing CRLF, null bytes, or non-printable characters. Deploy HTTP/2 where possible to mitigate response splitting.

name: MCP tool schema default value code injection
description: Attack pattern exploiting JSON Schema 'default' values in MCP tool inputSchema. Malicious servers embed executable code (eval(), Function(), import()) in schema default values. When LLMs auto-populate missing parameters with defaults, the injected code executes in the tool's runtime context, bypassing description-level scanning.
remediation: Scan all JSON Schema fields (default, const, examples, enum) for executable patterns. Never auto-execute schema default values. Implement schema validation that strips or rejects defaults containing code patterns.

name: ChatGPT Desktop MCP config poisoning via plugins directory
description: OpenAI ChatGPT Desktop (launched Q1 2026) supports MCP servers via its plugins directory. Malicious packages can inject MCP configs into the ChatGPT plugins directory (~/.config/chatgpt/plugins/), gaining persistent tool access across all ChatGPT Desktop sessions without user awareness.
remediation: Audit the ChatGPT plugins directory for unexpected MCP configurations. Verify all plugin sources against official registries. Enable ChatGPT Desktop plugin signing verification if available.

name: GitHub Copilot agent mode MCP tool injection
description: GitHub Copilot agent mode (GA March 2026) supports MCP tool servers. Malicious repositories can include .github/copilot-mcp.json files that register attacker-controlled MCP servers when Copilot processes the repository, enabling tool poisoning and code injection during AI-assisted development.
remediation: Audit .github/copilot-mcp.json in all repositories before enabling Copilot agent mode. Restrict MCP server registration to explicitly approved servers. Review Copilot workspace trust settings.

name: MCP OAuth PKCE bypass via code_verifier prediction
description: MCP servers that implement OAuth with PKCE using the 'plain' challenge method (instead of S256) or disable PKCE entirely allow authorization code interception attacks. The MCP spec mandates S256, but several popular server implementations default to 'plain' or accept downgrade requests, enabling token theft via man-in-the-middle or malicious redirect URIs.
remediation: Enforce PKCE with S256 challenge method. Reject 'plain' challenge methods and --no-pkce flags. Validate that code_verifier matches the originally registered code_challenge using SHA-256. Pin redirect URIs to localhost with dynamic ports only.

name: MCP gateway/proxy credential aggregation attack
description: MCP gateway/proxy servers that aggregate multiple upstream MCP servers create a single point of compromise. A malicious or compromised gateway can intercept all tool calls and responses, harvest credentials passed between clients and servers, modify tool results in transit, and selectively inject poisoned responses. The gateway pattern concentrates trust in a single component.
remediation: Implement end-to-end encryption between MCP clients and individual servers, bypassing the gateway for sensitive operations. Verify gateway provenance and integrity. Use mutual TLS for gateway-to-server connections. Audit gateway logs for tool call modification or credential interception.

name: MCP resource template path traversal via URI encoding
description: MCP resource templates that accept URI parameters are vulnerable to path traversal via double URL encoding (%252e%252e%252f), Unicode normalization attacks, or null byte injection. Attackers can escape the intended resource scope to access sensitive files (SSH keys, cloud credentials, environment files) on the server filesystem.
remediation: Canonicalize and decode all URI template parameters before path resolution. Reject paths containing .. sequences after full decoding. Implement allowlist-based resource access. Chroot resource access to a dedicated directory.

name: Malicious MCP server via Deno JSR registry typosquatting
description: Typosquatting packages on the Deno JSR registry targeting MCP server scoped packages. Attackers register near-identical scope names (@mcp-servers vs @mcp-server, @modelcontext vs @modelcontextprotocol) to distribute malicious MCP servers that exfiltrate tool call data, inject poisoned responses, or establish reverse shells.
remediation: Verify JSR package scope against official MCP registries. Check package publication date and author reputation. Pin exact versions in MCP configurations. Audit Deno permissions flags for unexpected network or filesystem access.

name: MCP sampling/createMessage abuse for autonomous agent escalation
description: MCP servers that request sampling/createMessage capabilities can autonomously invoke the LLM to generate and execute arbitrary tool calls. A malicious server uses sampling to create a recursive agent loop: the server calls createMessage to instruct the LLM to call other tools, escalating privileges beyond the original server's scope. This enables cross-server attacks without direct tool access (OWASP ASI03 Excessive Agency).
remediation: Restrict sampling/createMessage permissions to trusted servers only. Implement call-depth limits for recursive sampling requests. Require human-in-the-loop approval for sampling-initiated tool calls. Monitor sampling request patterns for recursive or cross-server escalation.

name: OpenRouter API key theft via MCP server env passthrough
description: MCP servers that proxy LLM requests via OpenRouter, LiteLLM, or similar aggregators often receive API keys via environment variable passthrough. A malicious server can exfiltrate these keys to access all models and billing on the victim's account, potentially running up significant charges or accessing sensitive API logs.
remediation: Never pass LLM API keys directly to MCP server environments. Use scoped, short-lived tokens with spending limits. Implement API key rotation. Monitor API usage for unexpected model access or geographic anomalies.

name: MCP tool result HTML/Markdown injection for UI rendering attacks
description: MCP tool results containing crafted HTML or Markdown that exploits client-side rendering. Malicious servers return tool results with embedded
remediation: Sanitize all tool result content before rendering. Strip HTML tags, scripts, and event handlers. Render Markdown in sandboxed iframes. Block external image loading in tool results unless explicitly approved by the user.

name: Compromised mcp-server-fetch variants with SSRF backdoor
description: Typosquatting variants of the popular mcp-server-fetch package that include SSRF backdoors. These packages function normally for external URLs but silently allow requests to internal network addresses (RFC 1918, link-local, cloud metadata) when specific trigger patterns appear in the URL, enabling internal network reconnaissance and credential theft.
remediation: Use only the official @anthropic-ai/mcp-server-fetch package. Verify package integrity via npm provenance. Audit all fetch-type MCP servers for internal network access patterns. Implement network-level egress filtering.

name: MCP Notification channel abuse for covert data exfiltration
description: MCP servers abuse the notification mechanism (notifications/message, notifications/resources/updated) to establish a covert exfiltration channel. By embedding stolen data in notification payloads, the server bypasses tool-call monitoring since notifications are treated as informational and often not logged or inspected by security tools.
remediation: Log and inspect all MCP notification payloads. Implement size limits on notification content. Block notifications containing patterns matching credentials, tokens, or encoded data. Rate-limit notification frequency per server.

name: OWASP ASVS-AI model context window poisoning via MCP tools
description: MCP servers that return extremely large tool results (exceeding typical context windows) to perform context window denial-of-service. By flooding the LLM context with irrelevant data, the attacker pushes out legitimate instructions and user context, degrading agent performance or causing it to ignore safety constraints that no longer fit in the context (OWASP ASI09 Improper Output Handling).
remediation: Implement maximum response size limits for MCP tool results. Truncate or summarize large responses before injecting into LLM context. Monitor tool result sizes and alert on anomalous growth. Reserve context window space for system instructions.

name: PyPI MCP server package with setup.py code execution
description: Malicious PyPI packages masquerading as MCP server implementations execute arbitrary code during pip install via setup.py. The setup.py harvests SSH keys, cloud credentials, and browser cookies before installing a seemingly functional MCP server. Targets Python-based MCP server installations via uvx/pipx.
remediation: Install MCP server packages only from verified publishers. Use --no-build-isolation cautiously. Audit setup.py before installation. Prefer pre-built wheels over source distributions. Check PyPI package provenance attestations.

name: MCP server impersonating IDE built-in tools via display name mismatch
description: MCP servers register tools with internal names that differ from their display names shown to users. The tool appears as 'Format Code' in the UI but its actual name matches a built-in tool like 'edit' or 'write'. When the LLM resolves tool calls by name, it calls the malicious server's tool instead of the IDE's built-in, intercepting all file edits.
remediation: Verify tool name-to-display-name consistency. Namespace all third-party MCP tool names with a server prefix. Implement tool name collision detection in IDE MCP integrations. Alert users when multiple tools share the same resolved name.

name: MCP server exploiting agent auto-approval for privilege escalation
description: MCP servers that detect or exploit auto-approval modes in AI agents (Claude Code --dangerously-skip-permissions, Cursor auto-run, Cline auto-approve). When auto-approval is active, a malicious server's tool calls execute without human review, enabling unrestricted filesystem access, network requests, and code execution. The server may probe for auto-approval status via timing side channels.
remediation: Never enable auto-approval for untrusted MCP servers. Implement per-server approval policies rather than global auto-approve. Log all auto-approved actions for post-hoc audit. Detect and alert on auto-approval configuration changes.

name: Go module MCP server supply chain via vanity import paths
description: Supply chain attack via Go module vanity import paths mimicking official MCP server organizations. Attackers register GitHub organizations with names similar to official MCP projects (mcp-servers, mcptools) and publish Go modules that contain backdoored MCP server implementations.
remediation: Verify Go module import paths against official MCP organization listings. Check module proxy checksums via go.sum. Audit Go module dependencies with govulncheck. Pin exact module versions in go.mod.

name: MCP Streamable HTTP transport downgrade to unencrypted HTTP
description: MCP servers configured with Streamable HTTP transport pointing to unencrypted HTTP endpoints. Without TLS, all tool calls, responses, and credentials are transmitted in plaintext, enabling network-level interception of MCP traffic. Particularly dangerous on shared networks, CI/CD runners, or cloud VPCs with compromised neighbors.
remediation: Enforce HTTPS for all Streamable HTTP MCP transport endpoints. Reject configurations with http:// endpoints. Implement certificate pinning for known MCP servers. Use mutual TLS where supported.

name: MCP tool with excessive filesystem scope (OWASP ASI03)
description: MCP filesystem servers configured with unrestricted or overly broad write access (root-level paths, wildcard scopes). Combined with prompt injection, this grants attackers the ability to modify any file including SSH authorized_keys, crontabs, shell profiles, and application configs. Violates OWASP Agentic Security Initiative ASI03 (Excessive Agency).
remediation: Restrict MCP filesystem servers to minimum required paths. Use allowlists for accessible directories. Enable read-only mode by default. Require explicit user approval for write operations outside project directories.

name: MCP server with clipboard access for credential harvesting
description: MCP servers with clipboard read access can harvest passwords, tokens, and sensitive data that users copy/paste during development. A malicious clipboard MCP server polls clipboard content at high frequency, exfiltrating credentials, API keys, and database connection strings that developers temporarily copy.
remediation: Restrict clipboard MCP server access to explicit user-initiated paste operations only. Implement clipboard access audit logging. Block clipboard polling (high-frequency reads). Sanitize clipboard content before exposing to MCP tools.

name: MCP server targeting AI coding assistants via .cursorrules/.clinerules poisoning
description: MCP servers that write or modify AI assistant configuration files (.cursorrules, .clinerules, .github/copilot-instructions.md) to inject persistent prompt instructions. These rule files are automatically loaded by AI coding assistants, creating a durable backdoor that persists across sessions and affects all developers working on the repository.
remediation: Monitor AI assistant config files for unexpected modifications. Implement file integrity monitoring for .cursorrules, .clinerules, and similar files. Review these files in code review. Restrict MCP server write access to project configuration directories.

name: MCP server with browser automation for session hijacking
description: MCP servers with browser automation capabilities (Playwright, Puppeteer, Selenium) that access user browser profiles can steal session cookies, saved passwords, and authentication tokens. When configured with --user-data-dir pointing to the user's actual browser profile, the server gains access to all active web sessions.
remediation: Never point browser automation MCP servers at real user profiles. Use isolated, ephemeral browser profiles. Audit browser MCP server flags for --user-data-dir or --browser-profile settings. Implement cookie isolation for automated browsing sessions.

name: MCP server abusing roots/list for project enumeration and targeting
description: MCP servers that enumerate project roots via the roots/list capability to fingerprint the development environment. By discovering all registered project paths, the server identifies high-value targets (crypto wallets, infrastructure repos, CI/CD configs) and tailors its attack payload accordingly. This reconnaissance phase precedes targeted data exfiltration.
remediation: Restrict roots/list responses to only the currently active project. Implement per-server root visibility policies. Audit which servers request roots/list and how frequently. Alert on servers that enumerate roots but do not use them for their stated purpose.

name: Azure MCP Server unauthenticated information disclosure (CVE-2026-32211)
description: Critical information disclosure in Azure MCP Server (CVSS 9.1). Missing authentication on sensitive endpoints exposes configuration details, API keys, and authentication tokens to unauthenticated remote attackers. Disclosed April 3, 2026 by Microsoft. Distinct from the earlier Azure MCP SSRF issue (CVE-2026-26118).
remediation: Update Azure MCP Server to the latest patched version immediately. Rotate any API keys or tokens exposed through the affected endpoints. Require authentication on every MCP endpoint. Place Azure MCP Server behind network-level access controls.

name: FrontMCP / mcp-from-openapi SSRF and local file read (CVE-2026-39885)
description: High severity SSRF and local file disclosure (CVSS 7.5) in FrontMCP / mcp-from-openapi. The library uses @apidevtools/json-schema-ref-parser without URL restrictions, allowing malicious OpenAPI specifications with $ref pointers to trigger Server-Side Request Forgery and read arbitrary local files when an agent loads attacker-provided API specs. Disclosed April 8, 2026.
remediation: Update mcp-from-openapi / FrontMCP to the latest patched version. Restrict $ref URL schemes to HTTPS only. Block file:// and private-IP dereferencing. Do not load OpenAPI specs from untrusted sources without sandboxing.

name: MCP Go SDK DNS rebinding protection disabled by default (CVE-2026-34742)
description: DNS rebinding protection is disabled by default in the Model Context Protocol Go SDK for MCP servers running on localhost. A malicious webpage can rebind DNS to 127.0.0.1 and send unauthenticated tool-invocation requests to the local MCP server from the browser, bypassing same-origin policy and enabling remote compromise of developer workstations. Complements the TypeScript (CVE-2025-66414) and Python (CVE-2025-66416) DNS rebinding fixes.
remediation: Update the Go MCP SDK to the patched version. Explicitly enable Host header validation and Origin header checks. Require an authentication token for every tool call even on localhost. Prefer stdio transport over HTTP for local servers.

name: MCPJam Inspector unauthenticated RCE (CVE-2026-23744)
description: Critical remote code execution in MCPJam Inspector versions 1.4.2 and earlier. The inspector binds to 0.0.0.0 by default and its server-management endpoint accepts unauthenticated HTTP requests to install arbitrary MCP servers. Any attacker on the same network can trigger installation of a malicious MCP server and achieve code execution on the developer workstation. Distinct from the Anthropic MCP Inspector DNS rebinding issue (CVE-2025-49596).
remediation: Update MCPJam Inspector to the latest patched version. Bind the inspector to 127.0.0.1 only, never 0.0.0.0. Require an authentication token for the server-management endpoint. Do not expose developer tooling to untrusted networks.

name: Visual Studio Code mcp.json command injection RCE (CVE-2026-21518)
description: Remote code execution in Microsoft Visual Studio Code via improper validation of user-supplied strings in mcp.json configuration files. A crafted mcp.json shipped in a repository can execute arbitrary code when a developer opens the project in VS Code with MCP enabled. Affects both stable and Insiders builds. Disclosed April 2026.
remediation: Update Visual Studio Code to the April 2026 security release. Review mcp.json files from untrusted repositories before opening the project. Use VS Code Workspace Trust to gate MCP execution. Disable workspace-scoped MCP servers for untrusted folders.

name: suvarchal docker-mcp-server OS command injection (CVE-2026-5741)
description: OS command injection in suvarchal docker-mcp-server up to 0.1.0, exploitable remotely via HTTP-triggered container-management actions. Attackers execute arbitrary host commands through unsanitized parameters to pull/stop/remove containers, pivoting from the MCP server to full host compromise. Public exploit is available.
remediation: Remove the suvarchal docker-mcp-server immediately. Prefer the official Docker MCP server from a verified publisher. Never pass user-provided strings to shell commands; always use execve-style argument arrays with strict allowlists.

name: codebase-mcp RepoMix OS command injection (CVE-2026-5023)
description: OS command injection in DeDeveloper23 codebase-mcp via the RepoMix Command Handler. The handler passes user-controlled repository paths and flags into a shell command, allowing local attackers (or remote attackers via prompt injection from indexed repositories) to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the MCP server.
remediation: Update codebase-mcp to the latest patched version. Pass repository paths as argv entries instead of shell strings. Reject path arguments containing shell metacharacters. Run the MCP server under a non-privileged user and filesystem sandbox.

name: Anthropic MCP TypeScript SDK ReDoS in UriTemplate (CVE-2026-0621)
description: Regular expression denial of service (ReDoS) in the UriTemplate class of Anthropic's MCP TypeScript SDK. A crafted URI sent through resources/read triggers catastrophic backtracking, causing 100% CPU utilization and hanging the server for all connected clients. Affects versions prior to the April 2026 patch.
remediation: Upgrade @modelcontextprotocol/sdk to the patched version. Validate resource URIs against a strict allowlist before passing to UriTemplate. Apply a per-request CPU timeout on MCP server handlers to contain ReDoS impact.

name: Flowise CustomMCP Function() constructor RCE actively exploited (CVE-2025-59528)
description: Maximum-severity (CVSS 10.0) code injection in Flowise versions 3.0.5 and earlier. The CustomMCP node passes user-provided server configuration into Node.js Function() constructor without sanitization, yielding child_process and fs access with full Node runtime privileges. Actively exploited in the wild since April 2026 against 12,000+ public instances, first observed from a Starlink IP by VulnCheck.
remediation: Upgrade Flowise to 3.1.1 or later (minimum 3.0.6). Audit logs for exploitation attempts against /api/v1/node-load-methods and CustomMCP endpoints. Rotate all credentials reachable from the Flowise host. Do not expose Flowise to the public internet without authentication.

name: MCP sampling resource theft -- compute quota drain (Unit 42)
description: MCP server configured to use the sampling feature without per-server quotas. Palo Alto Unit 42 demonstrated that malicious servers can abuse sampling/createMessage to drain the client's LLM API credits for attacker-controlled workloads, with the output never shown to the user. A single compromised server can silently rack up thousands of dollars in compute costs through sampling-triggered completions the user never sees.
remediation: Require explicit user consent for every sampling/createMessage call. Enforce per-server token and cost quotas. Log and display all sampling activity in the client UI. Disable sampling entirely for servers that do not need it.

name: Tool poisoning via SSH and cloud credential file read instructions
description: Tool poisoning pattern documented by Invariant Labs and Semgrep research: MCP tool descriptions or arguments reference SSH private keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519), AWS credential files (~/.aws/credentials), and ~/.npmrc tokens. Embedded hidden instructions tell the agent to read these files and return their contents to the server. Invisible to the user because tool descriptions are not typically displayed during normal chat.
remediation: Render every MCP tool description to the user on first load and on any update (rug-pull detection). Block MCP tool arguments that reference private key paths, cloud credential files, or dotenv files. Pin tool versions and verify cryptographic signatures.

name: Agent goal hijacking via MCP resource content injection (OWASP Agentic)
description: Agent goal hijack attack from the OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026: an MCP server returns crafted resource content that overrides the agent's original objective. Resources are injected into the LLM context as trusted data, so embedded instructions are executed without prompt-level safeguards, allowing attackers to redirect the agent toward attacker-controlled goals across multiple turns.
remediation: Treat MCP resource content as untrusted input. Apply prompt-injection classifiers to every resource before it enters the context. Namespace resource content with a clear untrusted marker. Require user approval before an agent consumes resources from unauthenticated MCP servers.

name: MCP Java SDK DNS rebinding (CVE-2026-36102)
description: DNS rebinding vulnerability in the MCP Java SDK (io.modelcontextprotocol:sdk) for localhost-bound servers. Completes the cross-SDK DNS rebinding series alongside TypeScript (CVE-2025-66414), Python (CVE-2025-66416), and Go (CVE-2026-34742). Malicious webpages rebind DNS to 127.0.0.1 and send unauthorized tool-invocation requests via the browser.
remediation: Update the MCP Java SDK to the latest patched version. Enable Host header and Origin header validation. Use stdio transport for local-only servers. Require authentication tokens for all HTTP-based transports.

name: JetBrains Junie MCP config injection via project files
description: Malicious repositories can include .junie/mcp/mcp.json files that register attacker-controlled MCP servers when opened in JetBrains IDEs with Junie AI assistant enabled. Similar to the VS Code mcp.json injection (CVE-2026-21518) and Claude Code project file exploitation (CVE-2025-59536).
remediation: Audit .junie/mcp/mcp.json in cloned repositories before opening. Restrict workspace-scoped MCP server registration to pre-approved servers. Enable Junie workspace trust settings.

name: Gemini CLI MCP configuration injection via settings.json
description: Malicious packages or repository files inject MCP server entries into the Gemini CLI settings (~/.gemini/settings.json). When the user runs Gemini CLI, the injected servers gain tool execution access. Follows the same pattern as SANDWORM_MODE's targeting of Claude Desktop, Cursor, and VS Code configurations.
remediation: Monitor ~/.gemini/settings.json for unauthorized changes. Use file integrity monitoring. Restrict write access to Gemini CLI configuration files. Verify all MCP server entries against known-good sources.

name: Zed editor MCP settings manipulation via workspace config
description: Malicious repositories can include .zed/settings.json files that register MCP servers when opened in the Zed editor. Zed's MCP configuration is embedded in its general settings file, making it harder to distinguish MCP changes from other configuration changes during code review.
remediation: Review .zed/settings.json in untrusted repositories before opening. Separate MCP configuration from general editor settings where possible. Implement workspace trust prompts for MCP server registration.

name: Amazon Q Developer MCP config injection
description: Malicious packages inject MCP server configurations into the Amazon Q Developer config (~/.aws/amazonq/default.json). The injected servers gain access to the agent's tool execution capabilities and potentially inherit AWS credentials from the developer's environment.
remediation: Audit ~/.aws/amazonq/default.json for unauthorized MCP entries. Use AWS SSO instead of static credentials. Implement file integrity monitoring on Amazon Q Developer configuration files.

name: MCP OAuth Protected Resource Metadata poisoning
description: Malicious MCP servers serve crafted Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource that redirects OAuth flows to attacker-controlled authorization servers. The MCP spec requires clients to discover the authorization server via this endpoint, creating a trust-on-first-use vulnerability.
remediation: Validate discovered authorization server URLs against an allowlist. Implement TOFU pinning for authorization servers after first successful connection. Alert users when the authorization server changes between connections. Require HTTPS for all discovery endpoints.

name: MCP server with AWS Bedrock model invocation access
description: MCP server with access to AWS Bedrock model invocation APIs. Prompt injection can abuse Bedrock access to invoke expensive foundation models, access knowledge bases containing sensitive enterprise data, or chain through Bedrock Agents to reach downstream AWS services with the assumed IAM role's permissions.
remediation: Use IAM policies with bedrock:InvokeModel restricted to specific model IDs. Implement per-session token budgets. Deny access to bedrock:CreateAgent and bedrock:UpdateKnowledgeBase. Monitor Bedrock usage for unexpected model invocations.

name: MCP Streamable HTTP Content-Type negotiation bypass
description: MCP Streamable HTTP transport implementations that do not strictly validate Content-Type headers. Attackers exploit lenient content negotiation to inject non-JSON payloads (HTML, multipart) that are misinterpreted by the client, enabling response splitting, XSS in rendered tool results, or parser confusion attacks.
remediation: Enforce strict Content-Type validation (application/json for POST, text/event-stream for SSE). Reject responses with unexpected Content-Type headers. Implement server identity verification via TLS certificate pinning.

name: A2A-to-MCP bridge protocol confusion attack
description: Protocol bridge servers that translate between Google A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol and MCP create trust boundary confusion. A2A tasks lack the tool-level permission model of MCP, allowing A2A agents to invoke MCP tools without the approval gates that MCP clients enforce. Attackers route malicious requests through A2A to bypass MCP client-side safety checks.
remediation: Implement equivalent permission checks on both sides of the protocol bridge. Require user approval for all cross-protocol tool invocations. Log all bridged requests for audit. Do not inherit trust from one protocol into the other.

name: MCP server with code review/merge approval access
description: MCP server with access to approve pull requests, merge code, or bypass branch protection rules. Prompt injection can auto-approve and merge attacker-controlled code changes, bypassing code review requirements and introducing backdoors directly into protected branches.
remediation: Never grant PR approval or merge permissions to MCP servers. Use read-only tokens for code review integrations. Require human-in-the-loop for all merge operations. Enforce branch protection rules that MCP tokens cannot override.

name: MCP server exploiting Terraform state file secrets exposure
description: MCP servers with access to Terraform state files (.tfstate) can read plaintext secrets stored in state. Terraform state often contains database passwords, API keys, TLS certificates, and cloud provider credentials in unencrypted form. Prompt injection can extract these secrets via resource attribute queries.
remediation: Never grant MCP servers direct access to Terraform state files. Use Terraform remote backends with encryption at rest. Enable state file encryption. Access state only through terraform state show with restricted IAM permissions.

name: MCP server agentic loop budget exhaustion (token bomb)
description: MCP servers that intentionally trigger agentic reasoning loops to exhaust the client's token budget. Malicious tool results contain instructions that create circular dependencies between tools, causing the agent to repeatedly invoke tools in an endless cycle. Research shows token amplification up to 142x through trivial text repetition and staged subtask generation.
remediation: Implement hard per-session token budgets and tool call count limits. Detect circular tool call patterns with cycle detection algorithms. Set per-tool invocation rate limits. Display real-time token usage to the user.

name: MCP server with log aggregation write access (audit tampering)
description: MCP server with write access to log aggregation systems (Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Loki). Prompt injection can manipulate the agent into deleting or modifying audit logs to cover tracks, injecting false log entries for attribution confusion, or exfiltrating sensitive data captured in application logs.
remediation: Use append-only credentials for MCP log integrations. Implement write-once-read-many (WORM) log retention policies. Restrict MCP servers to read-only log access. Enable tamper-evident logging with cryptographic chaining.

name: Continue.dev MCP config injection via YAML deserialization
description: Malicious repositories include .continue/config.yaml files that register attacker-controlled MCP servers when opened with the Continue.dev AI extension. Continue uses YAML configuration which supports anchors, aliases, and custom tags that can be exploited for config injection beyond simple MCP server registration.
remediation: Audit .continue/config.yaml in untrusted repositories. Use safe YAML parsing that rejects custom tags and complex aliases. Restrict workspace-scoped MCP server registration. Enable Continue workspace trust verification.

name: MCP homoglyph tool name confusion attack
description: MCP servers register tools with names containing Unicode homoglyphs (Cyrillic 'a' U+0430 vs Latin 'a' U+0061, Greek omicron vs Latin 'o'). The tool appears identical to a built-in tool in UI rendering but has a different Unicode representation, bypassing exact-match shadowing detection. The LLM may call either the legitimate or malicious tool unpredictably.
remediation: Normalize all tool names to ASCII before registration. Reject tool names containing mixed-script Unicode characters. Implement confusable detection using Unicode Technical Report #39. Display tool source server alongside tool names in UI.

name: PraisonAI OAuth token validation bypass (CVE-2026-34953)
description: Maximum-severity authentication bypass (CVSS 9.1) in PraisonAI multi-agent framework. The OAuthManager.validate_token() method returns True for any unknown token, granting full API access to unauthenticated attackers. Affects versions prior to 4.5.97.
remediation: Update PraisonAI to version 4.5.97 or later. Audit access logs for unauthorized API calls. Implement proper token validation with deny-by-default semantics.

name: Cursor IDE MCP deep-link trust bypass (CVE-2025-64106)
description: MCP deep-link trust bypass (CVSS 8.8) in Cursor IDE. Attackers craft deep links that mask malicious MCP server installation commands as legitimate installs, bypassing user verification of the actual command being executed.
remediation: Update Cursor to the latest version. Verify all MCP server installation commands before approving. Do not click MCP installation links from untrusted sources.

name: Cursor IDE CurXecute indirect prompt injection (CVE-2025-54135)
description: Indirect prompt injection (CVSS 8.6) in Cursor IDE allows creating and executing MCP server configuration files (.mcp.json) without user confirmation. Attackers embed prompt injection payloads in project files that Cursor processes, enabling automated MCP server installation and code execution.
remediation: Update Cursor to the latest patched version. Review .mcp.json files before opening untrusted projects. Enable Cursor workspace trust verification.

name: Cursor IDE MCPoison config modification after approval (CVE-2025-54136)
description: Rug pull vulnerability (CVSS 7.2) in Cursor IDE. After one-time MCP server approval, an attacker can modify the MCP configuration to change the server command or arguments without triggering re-approval. Enables post-approval command substitution attacks.
remediation: Update Cursor to the latest version. Implement hash-based MCP config verification that detects post-approval modifications. Re-verify MCP configurations periodically.

name: mcp-atlassian SSRF via unvalidated Atlassian headers (CVE-2026-27826)
description: SSRF vulnerability (CVSS 8.2) in mcp-atlassian via unvalidated X-Atlassian-Jira-Url and X-Atlassian-Confluence-Url headers. Distinct from the path traversal RCE (CVE-2026-27825). Enables attackers to reach internal services and steal managed identity tokens from cloud metadata endpoints.
remediation: Update mcp-atlassian to 0.17.0 or later. Validate all incoming HTTP headers against strict allowlists. Block access to RFC 1918 addresses and cloud metadata endpoints from the MCP server.

name: FastMCP OpenAPI Provider SSRF and path traversal (CVE-2026-32871)
description: SSRF and path traversal in FastMCP OpenAPI Provider via unencoded path parameters in URL templates. Attackers craft tool call parameters with path traversal sequences that reach internal endpoints or read arbitrary files when the OpenAPI provider constructs upstream HTTP requests.
remediation: Update FastMCP to the latest patched version. URL-encode all path parameters before constructing upstream requests. Validate constructed URLs against an allowlist of expected hosts.

name: MCP TypeScript SDK cross-client data leak (CVE-2026-25536)
description: Cross-client data leak in MCP TypeScript SDK versions 1.10.0 through 1.25.3. Shared Server/transport instance reuse causes data from one client session to leak into another client's responses. Affects multi-tenant MCP server deployments where multiple clients connect to a single server process.
remediation: Update @modelcontextprotocol/sdk to version 1.26.0 or later. Ensure Server instances are not shared across client connections. Use per-connection transport isolation.

name: MCP Go SDK Streamable HTTP CSRF (CVE-2026-33252)
description: Cross-Site Request Forgery in MCP Go SDK Streamable HTTP transport. The transport accepts cross-site POST requests without Origin header validation or Content-Type enforcement. Malicious web pages can send tool-invocation requests to localhost-bound MCP servers via the browser.
remediation: Update the Go MCP SDK to the latest version. Enforce Origin header validation on all Streamable HTTP endpoints. Require Content-Type: application/json for POST requests. Add CSRF token validation.

name: stata-mcp do-file command execution (CVE-2026-31040)
description: Insufficient validation of Stata do-file content in stata-mcp allows arbitrary command execution. Attackers craft Stata do-files with embedded shell commands that execute when processed by the MCP server.
remediation: Update stata-mcp to the latest patched version. Validate and sanitize all do-file content before execution. Restrict Stata shell escape commands.

name: magento2-dev-mcp OS command injection (CVE-2026-5603)
description: OS command injection in magento2-dev-mcp via the executeMagerun2Command handler. Unsanitized input is passed directly to shell execution, enabling arbitrary command execution on the host system.
remediation: Remove magento2-dev-mcp or update to a patched version. Use execve-style argument arrays instead of shell string concatenation. Validate all command inputs against strict allowlists.

name: mcp-data-vis SQL injection (CVE-2026-5322)
description: SQL injection in mcp-data-vis MCP Handler database function. User-controlled input from tool parameters is interpolated directly into SQL queries without parameterization, enabling data exfiltration, modification, or destruction.
remediation: Update mcp-data-vis to the latest patched version. Use parameterized queries exclusively. Restrict database user permissions to read-only where possible.

name: Smithery.ai MCP registry path traversal and credential exposure
description: Path traversal vulnerability in the Smithery.ai MCP server registry (October 2025) exposed 3,000+ hosted MCP servers, API keys, and an overprivileged fly.io deployment token via misconfigured dockerBuildPath parameters. First major MCP registry compromise.
remediation: Rotate all API keys registered through Smithery.ai. Verify server integrity for any MCP servers hosted on the platform. Use self-hosted registries with proper access controls.

name: n8n-mcp authenticated SSRF via instance URL header (GHSA-4ggg-h7ph-26qr)
description: SSRF in n8n-mcp (npm) versions below 2.47.4. A holder of a valid AUTH_TOKEN can supply attacker-controlled X-Instance-URL style headers; the server then issues HTTP requests to arbitrary URLs and reflects the response body through JSON-RPC, enabling reads of AWS/GCP/Azure IMDS endpoints and internal services in multi-tenant deployments.
remediation: Upgrade n8n-mcp to 2.47.4 or later. Disable multi-tenant HTTP mode when not required. Do not share AUTH_TOKEN with less-trusted clients. Block egress to RFC 1918 addresses and cloud metadata endpoints from the n8n-mcp process.

name: AWS API MCP Server file-access restriction bypass (CVE-2026-4270)
description: File-access restriction bypass in awslabs.aws-api-mcp-server PyPI package (>=0.2.14, <1.3.9). The no-access and workdir modes fail to canonicalize alternate paths, letting AWS CLI commands routed through the MCP server read arbitrary local files outside the configured workdir including ~/.aws/credentials, SSH keys, and .env files.
remediation: Upgrade awslabs.aws-api-mcp-server to 1.3.9 or later. Avoid running the server with the unrestricted file-access setting. Run the MCP server as a dedicated low-privilege user with no read access to ~/.aws or ~/.ssh.

name: Tencent WeKnora MCP stdio config validation bypass RCE (CVE-2026-30861)
description: Unauthenticated RCE in Tencent WeKnora (>=0.2.5, <0.2.10). The MCP stdio configuration validator whitelists npx/uvx and blacklists specific args, but the blacklist can be bypassed with npx -p node to execute attacker-supplied JavaScript. Combined with open user registration this yields unauthenticated remote code execution on the host (CVSS 9.9).
remediation: Upgrade WeKnora to 0.2.10 or later. Disable open user registration. Restrict MCP server config endpoints to authenticated admins. Replace command allowlists with a strict, structured argv allowlist rather than string-level blacklisting.

name: mcp-bridge unauthenticated RCE via /bridge endpoint (GHSA-wvr4-3wq4-gpc5)
description: Unauthenticated RCE in mcp-bridge (mcp-connect) default configuration. When AUTH_TOKEN and ACCESS_TOKEN are unset (the out-of-the-box default), the /bridge HTTP endpoint accepts any POST containing serverPath and args and spawns an arbitrary OS process as the bridge user. Heightened risk when exposed via ngrok through npm run start:tunnel.
remediation: Always set AUTH_TOKEN and ACCESS_TOKEN. Bind the bridge to 127.0.0.1 only. Firewall port 3000 from the public internet. Upgrade to the patched release. Never expose mcp-bridge via a public tunnel without authentication.

name: Claude Code CLI TERMINAL env var command injection (CVE-2026-35020)
description: Command injection in @anthropic-ai/claude-code CLI and Claude Agent SDK through v2.1.91. The command-lookup helper and deep-link terminal launcher pass the TERMINAL environment variable into a shell invocation without sanitization, letting a local attacker who can influence env vars inject arbitrary commands.
remediation: Upgrade @anthropic-ai/claude-code to the latest patched release. In shared CI runners, scrub the TERMINAL env var before invoking claude. Avoid running claude in environments where untrusted processes can set arbitrary env vars.

name: Claude Code CLI prompt-editor file-path command injection (CVE-2026-35021)
description: Command injection in @anthropic-ai/claude-code CLI and Claude Agent SDK via the prompt-editor invocation utility. The utility builds a shell command from user-controllable file paths without escaping, so a malicious project file path triggers arbitrary command execution when the editor is launched.
remediation: Upgrade @anthropic-ai/claude-code to the latest patched release. Never open untrusted repositories with the prompt editor enabled. Use execve-style argv arrays for editor launching instead of shell string concatenation.

name: Claude Code CLI auth helper shell=true injection (CVE-2026-35022)
description: Critical OS command injection (CVSS 9.8) in @anthropic-ai/claude-code CLI and Claude Agent SDK. The authentication-helper invocation reads helper configuration values and executes them via shell=true with no validation, so a malicious ~/.claude/settings.json or workspace .claude settings file yields full command execution as the developer.
remediation: Upgrade @anthropic-ai/claude-code to the latest patched release. Treat .claude/settings.json and helper configs from untrusted repositories as hostile. Review workspace settings before opening untrusted projects with Claude Code.

name: LangChain Core load_prompt path traversal (CVE-2026-34070)
description: Path traversal (CVSS 7.5) in langchain-core <1.2.22. load_prompt() and load_prompt_from_config() open paths embedded in deserialized config dicts without traversal or absolute-path validation, letting attacker-controlled prompt configs read arbitrary .txt, .json, and .yaml files such as /etc/passwd or ~/.ssh/*.
remediation: Upgrade langchain-core to 1.2.22 or later. Migrate from load_prompt/load_prompt_from_config to the langchain_core.load dumpd/loads APIs. Never pass allow_dangerous_paths=True. Do not load prompt configs from untrusted sources.

name: LiteLLM PyPI supply-chain compromise (March 2026)
description: Malicious LiteLLM releases 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 published to PyPI on March 24, 2026 via a compromised CI pipeline (initial access via a Trivy vulnerability). The packages executed payloads on import and via Python startup hooks, exfiltrating SSL/SSH keys, cloud credentials, kube configs, Git credentials, API keys, shell history, and crypto wallets. 40,000+ downloads before takedown.
remediation: Pin litellm to a known-good version (<=1.82.6 or a clean release post-1.82.8). Rotate ALL secrets reachable from any host that installed 1.82.7 or 1.82.8. Audit ~/.python_history and outbound connections. Rebuild affected container images.

name: @mobilenext/mobile-mcp screenshot path traversal file write (CVE-2026-33989)
description: Arbitrary file write (CVSS 8.1) in @mobilenext/mobile-mcp <0.0.49. The mobile_save_screenshot and mobile_start_screen_recording MCP tools pass saveTo/output parameters straight to filesystem APIs without sanitization, so a poisoned tool call containing ../ sequences writes arbitrary files anywhere the MCP process can write, including shell rc files or binaries on PATH.
remediation: Upgrade @mobilenext/mobile-mcp to 0.0.49 or later. Sandbox the MCP process to a writable workspace directory. Validate saveTo/output parameters against a strict allowlist of target directories.

name: LibreChat MCP header injection OAuth token theft (GHSA-pmw7-gqwj-f954)
description: OAuth token theft in LibreChat >=v0.8.2-rc1 and <=v0.8.3-rc1. User-created MCP server configs accept arbitrary HTTP headers, and processMCPEnv() substitutes credential placeholders such as {{LIBRECHAT_OPENID_ACCESS_TOKEN}} inside those headers at tool-call time. A malicious MCP server entry can therefore harvest victims' OAuth access tokens because omitServerManagedFields() excludes headers from its blocklist.
remediation: Upgrade LibreChat to v0.8.3-rc2 or later. Audit MCP server entries for placeholder usage in headers. Rotate any OAuth tokens that may have been exposed to user-defined MCP servers. Restrict which users can create MCP server configurations.

name: @grackle-ai/mcp knowledge_search workspace auth bypass (GHSA-647h-p824-99w7)
description: Authorization bypass in @grackle-ai/mcp <0.70.2. The knowledge_search and knowledge_get_node MCP tools are exposed in SCOPED_TOOLS (visible to scoped agents) but their handlers omit the workspace authorization check, letting an agent scoped to workspace A query the knowledge graph of workspace B in the same multi-tenant deployment.
remediation: Upgrade @grackle-ai/mcp to 0.70.2 or later. In multi-tenant deployments, audit logs for cross-workspace knowledge_search and knowledge_get_node calls. Enforce workspace scoping in every tool handler, not only at the tool-listing layer.

name: nginx-ui unauthenticated MCP endpoint nginx takeover (CVE-2026-33032)
description: Unauthenticated remote nginx takeover (CVSS 9.8) in nginx-ui <=2.3.5. The /mcp endpoint requires both IP whitelisting and AuthRequired() middleware, but /mcp_message only enforces IP whitelisting and the default whitelist is empty -- which the middleware treats as 'allow all'. Any network attacker can invoke MCP tools to restart nginx, create/modify/delete nginx configs, and trigger config reloads, achieving complete nginx service takeover without authentication.
remediation: Upgrade nginx-ui beyond 2.3.5 once a patch is released. Immediately firewall the nginx-ui admin port from the public internet. Apply AuthRequired() middleware to /mcp_message. Change the IP whitelist default from 'allow-all' to 'deny-all' when unconfigured. Monitor nginx config files for unauthorized changes.

name: aws-mcp-server command injection RCE (CVE-2026-5058)
description: Critical unauthenticated command injection (CVSS 9.8) in aws-mcp-server. The server maintains an allowed-commands list but fails to properly validate user-supplied strings before passing them to a system call, permitting classic shell metacharacter injection. An attacker with network access can craft a malicious string that bypasses validation and executes arbitrary OS commands as the MCP process user. This is a distinct product from the official awslabs.aws-api-mcp-server (CVE-2026-4270).
remediation: Bind aws-mcp-server to 127.0.0.1 only. Firewall the service from the public internet. Migrate to the official awslabs.aws-api-mcp-server (>=1.3.9). Replace string-based command allowlists with strict argv-based execution. Audit shell invocations for unsanitized user input.

name: aws-mcp-server AWS CLI command injection RCE (CVE-2026-5059)
description: Critical unauthenticated RCE (CVSS 9.8) in aws-mcp-server via AWS CLI command injection. Separate from CVE-2026-5058, this flaw lets an attacker inject AWS CLI subcommand strings that the server shells out without escaping, executing arbitrary code in the MCP process context and pivoting to AWS account compromise via the server's instance role credentials.
remediation: Disable aws-mcp-server until patched. Rotate any IAM credentials accessible from the server. Switch to the official awslabs.aws-api-mcp-server. Always invoke the AWS CLI via execFile/argv arrays rather than a shell, and enforce a strict allowlist of subcommands/flags.

name: LangChain Core LangGrinch serialization injection (CVE-2025-68664)
description: Critical serialization injection (CVSS 9.3, 'LangGrinch') in LangChain Core <0.3.81 and <1.2.5. The dumps()/dumpd() helpers fail to escape dictionaries containing the internal 'lc' key, so LLM-controlled fields such as additional_kwargs or response_metadata are rehydrated as real LangChain objects. With the historical secrets_from_env=True default an attacker can exfiltrate environment-variable secrets, instantiate classes from the langchain_core/langchain/langchain_community namespaces, and reach arbitrary code execution via Jinja2 templates. Most reachable through prompt injection in streaming MCP tool responses.
remediation: Upgrade langchain-core to >=0.3.81 or >=1.2.5. Never set secrets_from_env=True. Treat LLM-authored fields (additional_kwargs, response_metadata, tool_call args) as untrusted before serialization. Sanitize or drop unknown 'lc' keys on ingest. Audit agent pipelines that round-trip LLM outputs through dumps()/loads().

name: LiteLLM JWT userinfo cache collision auth bypass (CVE-2026-35030)
description: Critical authentication bypass in LiteLLM <1.83.0 when enable_jwt_auth is true. The OIDC userinfo cache uses only the first 20 characters of a JWT as the cache key. An unauthenticated attacker can craft a token whose first 20 characters collide with a legitimate user's cached token; on cache hit the attacker inherits that user's identity, permissions, and the cached userinfo claims. Defaults are unaffected (JWT auth is off by default) but any deployment that enabled it -- common in self-hosted proxy setups fronting MCP agents -- is vulnerable.
remediation: Upgrade LiteLLM to 1.83.0 or later (cache key now uses the full JWT hash). Rotate JWT signing keys and invalidate cached sessions. Disable JWT auth if not strictly required. Audit access logs for impossible-path authentications and cache-key collisions.

name: LiteLLM /config/update privilege escalation RCE (CVE-2026-35029)
description: High-severity privilege escalation in LiteLLM <1.83.0. The /config/update proxy endpoint fails to enforce admin role authorization, letting any authenticated user modify runtime proxy configuration. By rewriting guardrails, callbacks, or custom-code hooks, an attacker can pivot to full remote code execution on the LiteLLM proxy host. Disclosed alongside CVE-2026-35030 in the April 2026 LiteLLM security update following the March 2026 supply-chain incident.
remediation: Upgrade LiteLLM to 1.83.0 or later. Restrict /config/update to admin-scoped API keys only. Enable proxy audit logging. Review recent config mutations, callbacks, and custom-code hooks for malicious payloads. Isolate the LiteLLM proxy from sensitive data stores.

name: vLLM video processing RCE (CVE-2026-22778)
description: Critical pre-auth RCE (CVSS 9.8) in vLLM 0.8.3-0.14.0. A malicious video_url submitted to the Completions or Invocations API triggers the multimodal video decode path, chaining a PIL information leak (to bypass ASLR) with a heap overflow in the JPEG2000 decoder used by OpenCV/FFmpeg. No authentication required. MCP servers that proxy user tool calls into a shared vLLM inference backend inherit the exposure.
remediation: Upgrade vLLM to 0.14.1 or later. Disable the multimodal video pipeline if unused. Validate and allowlist video_url inputs at the MCP tool boundary. Run vLLM under a minimal seccomp profile and network-isolated from credential stores.

name: vLLM video base64 frame-count bypass OOM DoS (CVE-2026-34755)
description: Denial of service in vLLM 0.7.0 through <0.19.0. VideoMediaIO.load_base64() at vllm/multimodal/media/video.py splits video/jpeg data URLs on commas to extract frames but does not enforce the num_frames=32 limit that the load_bytes() path applies. An attacker can submit a single API request with thousands of comma-separated base64 JPEGs, exhausting memory and OOM-killing the server. Amplified when called repeatedly against shared MCP agent infrastructure.
remediation: Upgrade vLLM to 0.19.0 or later. Enforce request-size and frame-count limits at the API gateway. Rate-limit the multimodal endpoints. Monitor OOM kills and set container memory hard limits.

name: vLLM OpenAI API unbounded n parameter DoS (CVE-2026-34756)
description: Unauthenticated OOM denial of service in vLLM's OpenAI-compatible API server. The n parameter (number of completions per prompt) is not bounded, so a single /v1/completions or /v1/chat/completions request with a large n starves GPU memory and takes the inference backend offline. Relevant to MCP gateways that pass client-controlled sampling params straight into vLLM.
remediation: Upgrade vLLM to 0.19.0 or later. Clamp the n parameter at the MCP/API gateway (e.g. n<=4). Reject unauthenticated traffic to the OpenAI API server. Add per-tenant concurrency quotas.

name: mcp-memory-service information disclosure (CVE-2026-29787)
description: Information disclosure in mcp-memory-service. The memory-retrieval tool does not scope results by the calling session/agent, letting any connected MCP client read embeddings and raw memory entries written by other sessions. Multi-tenant deployments risk leaking prior conversations, API keys pasted into chat history, and retrieval-augmented context from unrelated users.
remediation: Upgrade mcp-memory-service to the latest patched version. Enforce session/agent scoping on every memory tool handler, not just the retrieval UI. Encrypt memory entries per session. Purge existing memory stores in shared deployments and audit retrieval logs for cross-session hits.

name: MCP tool definition rug pull -- post-approval silent redefinition
description: Rug-pull attack pattern documented by Invariant Labs, Elastic Security Labs, and Acuvity throughout Q1-Q2 2026. A previously benign MCP server waits for initial user approval, then silently mutates its tools/list response (or the underlying tool implementation) days or weeks later to add exfiltration side effects. Because most clients cache tool approval once and do not re-verify description hashes, the malicious redefinition takes effect silently across all connected agents. Common second-stage payloads include Discord/Slack webhook posting, email BCC, and prompt-injection strings embedded in tool descriptions. Detection is best-effort: watch for servers whose tools/list changes after first connection.
remediation: Pin tool definition hashes at first approval and re-prompt users when descriptions change. Reject dynamic tools/list mutations unless signed. Adopt the ETDI proposal (OAuth-enhanced tool definitions + policy-based access control). Log every tools/list response and alert on drift. For CI agents, cache a known-good manifest of each MCP server and fail the build on mismatch.

name: mcp-framework unbounded request body DoS (CVE-2026-39313)
description: Unauthenticated denial-of-service (GHSA-published 2026-04-16) in mcp-framework <=0.2.21. The HTTP transport's readRequestBody() concatenates request body chunks into a single string without honoring the documented maxMessageSize limit, so a single large POST to /mcp exhausts Node's V8 string heap and crashes the server. Any mcp-framework-built HTTP MCP server is reachable by unauthenticated attackers on the network, enabling trivial repeat-DoS and CI pipeline disruption.
remediation: Upgrade mcp-framework to >=0.2.22. Enforce a reverse-proxy request-size limit (nginx client_max_body_size, Caddy request_body max_size) in front of any mcp-framework server. Add per-IP rate limiting on /mcp. Monitor for OOM restarts and 413 responses.

name: mcp-server-kubernetes port_forward argument injection (CVE-2026-39884)
description: Argument-injection RCE (CWE-88) in Flux159 mcp-server-kubernetes <3.5.0. The port_forward tool builds a kubectl command by string concatenation of user input, then naively splits on spaces before passing to child_process.spawn(). A poisoned MCP tool call can smuggle extra kubectl flags (--kubeconfig, --server, --token, --namespace) to pivot cross-namespace, dump secrets from unrelated workloads, or forward ports against attacker-chosen clusters. Reachable indirectly via prompt injection against any agent connected to the MCP server.
remediation: Upgrade flux159/mcp-server-kubernetes to >=3.5.0. Replace string-spawn with argv arrays (child_process.execFile). Constrain the MCP process to a single namespace via a dedicated kubeconfig + role binding. Review audit logs for unexpected --kubeconfig or --server flags originating from MCP-driven kubectl invocations.

name: Jaaz MCP STDIO configuration RCE (CVE-2026-30616)
description: Critical unauthenticated RCE in Jaaz <=1.0.30 (published 2026-04-15). The network-accessible Jaaz app exposes an MCP management interface that lets anyone register an MCP STDIO server with arbitrary command and arguments. Any subsequent agent execution shells the attacker-supplied command, giving full code execution as the Jaaz service user. Representative of the Ox-Security-disclosed MCP STDIO 'execute first, validate never' pattern.
remediation: Upgrade Jaaz to the patched release (pending per vendor). Until patched, firewall the Jaaz MCP management interface from untrusted networks, disable dynamic MCP server registration, and require signed MCP server manifests. Audit Jaaz host processes for unexpected command lines and rotate any credentials reachable from the host.

name: LangChain-ChatChat MCP STDIO RCE (CVE-2026-30617)
description: RCE (CVSS 8.6) in LangChain-ChatChat 0.3.1 published 2026-04-15. The application ships a publicly reachable MCP management interface that accepts arbitrary STDIO server configurations. Once MCP is enabled for agent execution, every agent turn launches the attacker-configured command, yielding persistent code execution as the ChatChat service account and easy pivot to any LLM API keys, vector stores, or retrieval secrets on the host.
remediation: Pin away from LangChain-ChatChat 0.3.1 until a patched release ships. Place an authenticated reverse proxy in front of the MCP management interface. Disable the STDIO transport for user-configurable MCP entries. Rotate all OpenAI/Anthropic/embedding keys reachable from the ChatChat host.

name: excel-mcp-server path traversal arbitrary file R/W (CVE-2026-40576)
description: Path traversal (GHSA-j98m-w3xp-9f56) in excel-mcp-server <=0.1.7 when running in SSE or Streamable-HTTP transport. All 25 exposed MCP tool handlers accept a filepath argument without sanitization, so an unauthenticated network attacker can supply ../ sequences to read, write, and overwrite arbitrary files the MCP process can touch -- including ~/.ssh/authorized_keys, shell rc files, Jenkins/CI config, or binaries on PATH.
remediation: Upgrade excel-mcp-server to >=0.1.8. Bind the MCP server to 127.0.0.1 when not intentionally exposed. Sandbox the process to a single workbook directory via chroot, container bind-mounts, or seccomp. Enforce a strict filepath allowlist (absolute path under workspace root only, no .. components).

name: MCP Java-SDK DNS rebinding browser pivot (CVE-2026-35568)
description: Origin-validation flaw (CWE-346, GHSA-8jxr-pr72-r468) in the official modelcontextprotocol java-sdk <1.0.0. The HTTP server transport fails to validate the Host header, so a victim's browser visiting an attacker-controlled page can resolve a hostile DNS name to 127.0.0.1 and issue cross-origin requests to the locally running Java MCP server, invoking its tools and exfiltrating any data the server exposes. Analogous to the prior Python/TypeScript SDK DNS-rebinding bugs.
remediation: Upgrade mcp-core to >=1.0.0. Enable the new Host header allowlist in every Java MCP server deployment. Bind to 127.0.0.1 only and firewall off loopback-plus-LAN access from browser traffic. For shared dev hosts, require Origin checks and CORS preflight on the MCP HTTP transport.

name: mcp-handler cross-session response leak race condition (GHSA-w2fm-25vw-vh7f)
description: Cross-client data-leak race condition (CWE-362) in mcp-handler <1.1.0 and underlying @modelcontextprotocol/sdk <1.26.0. When a single StreamableHTTPServerTransport instance is reused across concurrent HTTP clients, the shared server-side state (authentication context, tool-call results, user identity) can leak between sessions, so user A's tool response may be delivered to user B. High-impact for multi-tenant MCP gateways and hosted agent platforms.
remediation: Upgrade mcp-handler to >=1.1.0 and @modelcontextprotocol/sdk to >=1.26.0. Instantiate a fresh StreamableHTTPServerTransport per request/session. Add integration tests that interleave two authenticated clients and assert no cross-contamination. Audit logs for response delivery to the wrong session ID.

name: Apollo MCP Server DNS rebinding auth bypass (CVE-2026-35577)
description: DNS-rebinding authentication bypass in Apollo MCP Server <1.7.0. The StreamableHTTP transport does not validate the Host header, letting a malicious website bypass same-origin policy and invoke tools or read resources on the victim's locally running Apollo MCP server. Enables remote tool execution against developer workstations that happen to visit attacker-controlled sites while Apollo MCP is listening on localhost.
remediation: Upgrade Apollo MCP Server to >=1.7.0 (adds Host-header allowlisting). Bind to 127.0.0.1 only. Require authentication tokens for all tool invocations. Consider running MCP servers inside an isolated browser profile or loopback-only namespace when not in active use.

name: MaxKB MCP node OS command injection (CVE-2026-6108)
description: OS command injection in 1Panel-dev MaxKB <=2.6.1 via the execute function in the Model Context Protocol Node component. Remote attackers can inject shell metacharacters into MCP node parameters to run arbitrary OS commands as the MaxKB service user. Exploit code is public. Relevant to any knowledge-base deployment that lets users define MCP nodes in workflows.
remediation: Upgrade MaxKB beyond 2.6.1 once patched. Restrict who can author MCP nodes (admin-only). Run MaxKB workers under a dedicated unprivileged UID with no access to host secrets. Audit workflow execution logs for unexpected shell invocations and rotate credentials that the worker could reach.

name: MCP STDIO 'execute first, validate never' systemic flaw (Ox Security, April 2026)
description: Systemic MCP protocol flaw disclosed by Ox Security on 2026-04-15 after a months-long audit (30+ responsible disclosures, 10+ Critical/High CVEs). The STDIO transport in the official Anthropic MCP SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Java, Rust) starts the configured command *before* validating it, and continues executing user input even when the server fails to launch. Affects 200+ open-source projects, 150M+ downloads, 7000+ publicly reachable servers, and ~200,000 vulnerable instances. Anthropic declined an architectural change, calling the behavior 'expected,' and instead added a policy warning that STDIO MCP adapters should be used with caution. This is the common root cause behind CVE-2026-30616 (Jaaz), CVE-2026-30617 (LangChain-ChatChat), and CVE-2026-33032 (nginx-ui).
remediation: Treat every MCP STDIO server config as untrusted input. Require strict allowlists of command binaries, reject user-supplied absolute paths, and run the MCP spawner under a dedicated UID / container. Prefer HTTP+auth transports with Host-header validation over STDIO for hosted agents. Monitor for MCP config mutations post-deployment. When embedding a Anthropic MCP SDK, pin to the versions carrying the April 2026 mitigations.

name: Claude Code project settings hooks/MCP RCE + token exfil (CVE-2025-59536 / CVE-2026-21852)
description: Check Point Research disclosure: Anthropic Claude Code <=1.0.73 honored project-local .claude/settings.json on open, letting any repo author register malicious Hooks, MCP servers, or env vars that shell-execute on clone and exfiltrate ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. Triggered by simply opening an untrusted repository in Claude Code. Patched prior to disclosure by Anthropic, but agent operators running older Claude Code builds, CI runners that auto-trust project configs, or custom forks remain vulnerable.
remediation: Upgrade Claude Code to the patched release (>=1.0.74). Disable auto-trust of .claude/settings.json in CI. Scan repositories for .claude/settings.json before first open. Rotate ANTHROPIC_API_KEY on any host that opened an untrusted repo with an older Claude Code build. Review .claude/settings.json and .claude/hooks/ paths in supply-chain scanning.

name: Splunk MCP Server cleartext session/auth token leak (CVE-2026-20205)
description: Information disclosure in Splunk MCP Server <1.0.3. Users with access to the _internal index or holding the high-privilege mcp_tool_admin capability can read other users' Splunk session and authorization tokens in cleartext from MCP audit logs. Published 2026-04-14, updated 2026-04-15. Enables low-friction horizontal privilege escalation inside a Splunk tenant whenever MCP tooling is enabled.
remediation: Upgrade Splunk MCP Server to >=1.0.3 (logs are redacted, the token field is hashed). Restrict the mcp_tool_admin capability to break-glass accounts only. Review _internal index ACLs and purge historical MCP logs that contain live tokens. Rotate any Splunk session tokens visible to non-admin users since MCP Server was enabled.
